proposals.title: Directional Adhesion on Vertical Micropillars via Tip Morphology and Magnetic Actuation for Surface Climbing Microrobots
proposals.motivation: Directional dry adhesives inspired by gecko setae are crucial for climbing microrobots, but they typically rely on angled pillar arrays to generate anisotropic adhesion. This angled geometry complicates manufacturing, limits material choice, and can reduce durability. Investigating if vertical pillars can achieve similar performance would simplify fabrication and enable new designs for robust, small-scale climbing robots in inspection and biomedical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that vertical elastomeric micropillars, when equipped with asymmetric tip geometries or magnetically actuatable soft tips, can generate controllable, directional adhesion forces comparable to angled pillars, decoupling structural orientation from functional anisotropy.
proposals.method: We will fabricate vertical polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) micropillar arrays via soft lithography. Two tip modifications will be tested: 1) Asymmetric mushroom tips with offset caps, and 2) Tips infused with magnetic microparticles to create soft, compliant caps. Directional adhesion will be characterized using a custom micro-tribometer under controlled preload, shear, and pull-off cycles. For magnetic tips, we will apply external magnetic fields to dynamically alter tip stiffness and contact area, mimicking the engagement/disengagement of angled pillars. The performance (adhesion strength, anisotropy ratio, durability) will be compared directly to traditional angled-pillar adhesives.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for directional dry adhesives, potentially simplifying their integration into microrobotic systems. It will provide a quantitative framework for comparing adhesion mechanisms and could lead to more durable and manufacturable climbing surfaces for small robots in confined environments.
proposals.novelty: While angled pillars are the standard, this work uniquely challenges that geometry by combining tip-shape asymmetry with active magnetic softening—a method explored for locomotion but not for creating directional adhesion on vertical structures—to achieve controllable anisotropic adhesion.

proposals.title: Tunable Multi-Modal Adhesion for Surface-Adaptive Microrollers in Biologically Wet Environments
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, microrobots for biomedical applications, such as drug delivery or tissue inspection, primarily rely on dry van der Waals (vdW) adhesion for surface attachment, limiting reliable operation in the presence of physiological moisture. Combining vdW forces with capillary or electroadhesive mechanisms could enable robust, tunable adhesion across a wider range of surface conditions (dry, damp, wet), which is critical for transitioning microrobots from controlled dry environments to real-world biological settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid adhesion strategy, integrating vdW, capillary, and electroadhesive forces through a structured soft composite material, will provide superior, on-demand tunable adhesion force and reliability for magnetic microrollers on complex, moist surfaces compared to any single mechanism alone.
proposals.method: We will fabricate soft magnetic microrollers with a hierarchical surface architecture: a gecko-inspired microfibrillar array for vdW adhesion, impregnated with a hydrophilic polymer matrix to regulate capillary bridging, and coated with a thin, compliant conductive layer for electroadhesion. Using external magnetic fields for locomotion and rolling, we will independently control the electroadhesive voltage and the micro-roller's preload. Adhesion performance will be quantified on substrates with controlled humidity and surface energy using a micro-force sensor, comparing pure vdW, humidity-activated capillary, voltage-activated electroadhesion, and combined modes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design framework and proof-of-concept for surface-adaptive microrobots capable of maintaining strong adhesion in variable moisture conditions. It will provide the first quantitative model for the interplay and switching between these adhesion modes in a rolling microrobot, directly addressing a key barrier for in vivo medical microrobotics.
proposals.novelty: While multi-modal locomotion is common, the deliberate integration and active tuning of complementary *intermolecular* (vdW), *liquid-mediated* (capillary), and *field-induced* (electroadhesive) adhesion mechanisms on a single, mobile soft microrobot is a novel approach to achieve environmental robustness.

proposals.title: Active Gecko-Inspired Adhesives: Shape Memory Polymer Pillars for On-Demand Attachment and Release in Climbing Microrobots
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 climbing and perching robots, including those inspired by gecko adhesion, rely on passive elastomer pillars whose adhesive properties are fixed by their static geometry. This limits their adaptability to diverse surfaces and complicates controlled detachment, which is crucial for dynamic locomotion and energy efficiency in small-scale robots. A material system that can actively reconfigure its contact geometry would enable robust, reversible adhesion on demand, expanding the operational envelope of climbing microrobots for inspection and drug delivery tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that micropillar arrays fabricated from thermally actuated shape memory polymers (SMPs) can provide switchable adhesion by dynamically altering their tip geometry and contact area in response to a localized thermal stimulus, enabling controlled attachment and energy-efficient release.
proposals.method: We will fabricate SMP (e.g., polyurethane-based) micropillar arrays using two-photon polymerization lithography, tailoring the glass transition temperature (Tg) for actuation via integrated microheaters or external IR light. A magnetic soft composite layer will be integrated at the pillar base, enabling remote robot locomotion and positioning using pre-2026 magnetic control systems. Adhesion performance (shear/pull-off forces) will be quantified on standardized surfaces while cycling the SMP between its temporary (collapsed, low adhesion) and permanent (erect, high adhesion) shapes. A feedforward control algorithm will coordinate thermal actuation with robot motion phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of active, stimuli-responsive dry adhesives for soft robotics. It will provide quantitative models for adhesion force as a function of SMP pillar geometry and actuation state. The demonstrated integration with a magnetically driven platform will advance towards fully functional climbing microrobots with decoupled locomotion and adhesion mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While SMPs have been used for macroscopic shape change, their application as the active element in gecko-inspired microfibrillar adhesives for direct adhesion modulation is unexplored. This moves beyond using SMPs as passive substrates or for gross morphology change, targeting precise, reversible control of the terminal contact interface itself.

proposals.title: Material-Specific Optimization of Bioinspired Fibrillar Adhesives via High-Throughput Fabrication and Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: Bioinspired fibrillar adhesives, inspired by gecko setae, show immense promise for climbing robots and medical devices. However, their performance is highly sensitive to pillar geometry and the target substrate material. Current design is largely empirical and lacks a systematic framework to tailor pillar diameter, height, and angle for optimal adhesion on specific materials like glass, silicone, or biological tissue, limiting their reliable application.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a given target material, there exists an optimal set of fibrillar geometric parameters (diameter, height, tilt angle) that maximizes the adhesion ratio (pull-off force to preload), and that this relationship can be captured by a predictive machine learning model trained on high-throughput experimental data.
proposals.method: We will fabricate arrays of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) micropillars with varying geometry using soft lithography and a tilted-stage technique for angled pillars. Adhesion performance will be characterized on a suite of target materials using a custom micro-force tester. A high-throughput experimental design (e.g., Latin Hypercube Sampling) will generate a comprehensive dataset. This data will train a Gaussian Process Regression model to map geometry and material properties (e.g., surface energy, modulus) to adhesion ratio, enabling inverse design.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a validated, predictive design tool for material-specific fibrillar adhesives, moving beyond trial-and-error. It will provide fundamental insights into the contact mechanics of structured adhesives across diverse surfaces. The resulting design rules will directly advance the development of more reliable climbing microrobots and soft grippers for heterogeneous environments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work explores single geometries, we systematically decouple and optimize multiple geometric degrees of freedom specifically for different target materials. The integration of high-throughput experimentation with machine learning for inverse design of fibrillar adhesives represents a significant methodological advance over existing parametric studies.

proposals.title: Magnetically Actuated Soft Micro-Pillars for Adaptive Micromanipulation and Targeted Drug Delivery
proposals.motivation: In pre-2026 microrobotics, a key challenge for biomedical applications is achieving safe, adaptive physical interaction with delicate, unstructured biological environments. Existing rigid microtools or magnetic microrollers can apply excessive local forces or lack the compliance needed for precise manipulation of cells or targeted drug release at tissue surfaces. Integrating soft robotics principles at the microscale can address this gap by enabling inherently safer and more conformable interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fabricating the contact elements of magnetic microrobots as magnetically actuated soft polymer pillars will provide tunable compliance and adaptive gripping, significantly improving the safety and effectiveness of micromanipulation and localized drug delivery compared to rigid counterparts.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate arrays of soft, elastomeric micro-pillars (diameter ~50-200 µm) doped with NdFeB microparticles. Using pre-2026 finite element modeling (e.g., COMSOL with magnetostriction modules), we will optimize pillar geometry and magnetization profiles for controlled bending and buckling under external magnetic fields. These soft pillars will be integrated as the end-effector on a magnetically guided mobile platform. We will characterize their performance in adaptive gripping of micro-objects and triggered release of drug payloads from a hydrogel matrix within the pillar structure, using high-speed microscopy and force sensing.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new design paradigm for compliant end-effectors in mobile microrobots, directly applicable to intraocular or gastrointestinal theranostics. It will provide quantitative frameworks for modeling soft magnetic micro-actuators and demonstrate a functional microrobot capable of both precise manipulation and localized, mechanically triggered drug release.
proposals.novelty: While magnetic soft composites exist, their application as structured, field-controllable *micro-pillar* grippers on mobile microrobots for combined manipulation and drug delivery is novel. This merges Sitti's expertise in magnetic actuation and microrollers with soft robotics principles for a unique, multifunctional micro-tool.

proposals.title: Magnetically Actuated, Hierarchical Adhesive Pads for Macroscopic Gripping of Heavy, Smooth Industrial Components
proposals.motivation: Handling large, heavy, and smooth objects like glass panels or polished metal sheets in automated industrial settings remains a significant challenge. Traditional suction or rigid grippers struggle with energy efficiency, surface damage, or fail in low-pressure environments. A robust, scalable, and reversible dry adhesive gripping technology is urgently needed to advance manufacturing and logistics automation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical adhesive structure, combining angled elastomeric microfiber arrays inspired by gecko setae with a soft, magnetically actuated backing layer, can achieve strong, controllable adhesion on smooth macroscopic surfaces, enabling the reliable gripping and release of heavy industrial payloads.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate large-scale (e.g., 10x10 cm) adhesive pads using a multi-step molding process to create angled polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) microfibers. This adhesive layer will be bonded to a soft composite backing embedded with magnetic microparticles (e.g., NdFeB). Using external electromagnetic coils, we will programmatically apply specific magnetic field sequences to induce controlled, global deformation of the backing layer, modulating preload and peel angles for optimal attachment and clean, low-force detachment. Gripping performance (shear/pull-off force, cycles) will be characterized on glass and anodized aluminum panels using a multi-axis load cell.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a functional prototype of a macroscale, dry adhesive gripper capable of handling objects exceeding 100 N, with a detailed model linking magnetic actuation parameters to adhesive performance. It will provide a scalable design and control framework for transitioning bio-inspired adhesives from small-scale robotics to heavy-duty industrial manipulation.
proposals.novelty: While angled microfibers and magnetic soft actuators are known separately, their integration into a single, macroscale system for industrial payloads is unexplored. The novelty lies in using programmable magnetic fields to dynamically control the engagement mechanics of a large-area hierarchical adhesive, a method distinct from prior work on passive or thermally activated gecko-inspired tapes.

proposals.title: Magnetically Actuated, Directionally Switchable Micropillar Adhesives for Reversible Climbing Robot Attachment
proposals.motivation: Climbing robots and temporary fixtures require reliable, reusable, and energy-efficient attachment mechanisms. Pre-2026 dry adhesives inspired by gecko setae offer excellent performance but often lack simple, fast, and reversible on-demand detachment without complex peeling motions or external tackifiers. Developing a surface that provides strong directional adhesion and easy, non-destructive release would significantly enhance the utility and operational lifespan of climbing microrobots and perching systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an array of soft magnetic elastomer micropillars, whose effective adhesion can be modulated via an external magnetic field, will provide strong, directional dry adhesion for load-bearing, and allow for rapid, reversible detachment by field-induced pillar reorientation, eliminating the need for complex peeling mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will fabricate arrays of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) micropillars doped with magnetic microparticles (e.g., NdFeB) using soft lithography. The pillar geometry (aspect ratio, tip shape) will be optimized for directional shear adhesion. An external electromagnetic coil system will generate a controlled, localized magnetic field. Adhesion performance (shear and normal forces) will be characterized with and without magnetic actuation using a microforce tester. The detachment will be triggered by applying a magnetic field to induce collective pillar bending or twisting, breaking the contact interface cleanly.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new class of actively switchable dry adhesives to the field of climbing robotics and reversible fastening. It will provide quantitative models for magnetic field-controlled adhesion modulation and demonstrate a functional prototype on a climbing robot platform, enabling new capabilities for energy-efficient perching and locomotion on vertical surfaces.
proposals.novelty: While magnetic actuation of soft structures is known, its specific application to create a *reversibly switchable* gecko-inspired adhesive surface—where the same field used for robot propulsion (e.g., magnetic microrollers) also controls attachment/detachment—is novel. This integrates Sitti's expertise in magnetic soft robots and dry adhesives into a unified, multifunctional surface.

proposals.title: Magnetically Programmable, Structurally Active Covalent Organic Frameworks for Untethered Microrobot Locomotion and Drug Delivery
proposals.motivation: Current microrobots often separate rigid structural bodies from discrete actuators (e.g., attached magnetic particles), limiting force transmission, miniaturization, and environmental adaptability. A material where the structural matrix itself actuates would enable more efficient, robust, and multifunctional agents for biomedical tasks like targeted drug delivery, as sought in pre-2026 intraocular theranostics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embedding anisotropic, high-density magnetic nanoparticle networks directly into the covalent bonds of a soft, porous Covalent Organic Framework (COF) will create a monolithic material whose structural lattice generates motive force under magnetic fields, eliminating the need for a separate actuator component.
proposals.method: We will synthesize biphasic COFs using pre-2026 methods, integrating superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs) as covalent nodes within the organic linker network to create a magneto-active porous scaffold. The COF's morphology (e.g., microroller, helical) will be defined via template-assisted synthesis. Actuation will be achieved using pre-characterized rotating magnetic fields, with locomotion performance (speed, traction) measured in biologically relevant fluids. Drug loading and release kinetics within the active COF matrix will be quantified.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of 'structurally active' materials for microrobotics, demonstrating a monolithic design where the drug-carrying body is also the prime mover. It will provide a framework for quantifying the trade-offs between structural integrity, actuation strain, and therapeutic payload in unified architectures.
proposals.novelty: While magnetic composites and COF drug carriers exist pre-2026, the novelty lies in using the COF's covalent lattice as the primary stress-generating element for locomotion, moving beyond surface-functionalized or embedded discrete actuators to achieve true structural actuation.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Limits of Dry Adhesion: The Role of Partial Contact in Gecko-Inspired Spatular Pads
proposals.motivation: Gecko-inspired dry adhesion, crucial for climbing robots and medical devices, is widely modeled assuming perfect, full contact of nanoscale spatular pads. However, real-world surfaces are rough and dynamic, making perfect contact improbable. Understanding the adhesion performance under imperfect contact is essential for designing robust, real-world adhesive systems for robotics and surface-interactive tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that effective dry adhesion can be maintained with only partial spatular contact, and that a critical, non-linear threshold of contact area exists below which adhesion performance degrades precipitously.
proposals.method: We will fabricate soft, elastomeric micropillar arrays with controlled tip geometries (flat, mushroom-shaped) mimicking spatulae. Using a precision micro-indentation and shear force stage, we will characterize adhesion and friction forces while systematically varying contact area via controlled tilt and preload. High-speed interference microscopy will visualize real-time contact mechanics. Magnetic actuation will be integrated to dynamically detach and reposition samples, simulating locomotion cycles. Data will be analyzed using a modified Kendall peel model incorporating measured contact fractions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental scaling law between partial contact area and adhesion force for bio-inspired pads. It will provide design guidelines for next-generation adhesives that are tolerant to surface imperfections and inform control strategies for climbing and perching robots, enhancing their reliability on diverse substrates.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes for maximum adhesion, we systematically investigate the failure limits and performance under deliberately imperfect conditions, bridging the gap between idealized models and practical robotic implementation. The integration of in-situ contact visualization with magnetic dynamic testing for partial contact is novel.

proposals.title: Temporal Dynamics of Adhesive Contact in Soft Microrobotic Systems: Investigating Creep and Interfacial Rearrangement
proposals.motivation: For small-scale robots performing tasks like perching, drug delivery, or surface locomotion, reliable adhesion is critical for function. Pre-2026 work in Sitti's lab and others often assumes adhesive force is constant during contact, but this may not hold for soft, viscoelastic materials or dynamic biological interfaces. Understanding time-dependent adhesion is essential for designing robust microrobots and predicting their performance in real-world applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effective adhesive strength between a soft microrobotic foot and a target surface is not constant but evolves over contact time due to material creep and molecular rearrangement at the interface, significantly impacting detachment mechanics.
proposals.method: We will fabricate soft, magnetically actuated adhesive footpads using polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) and hydrogel composites, inspired by prior microroller and gecko-inspired adhesive designs. A custom micro-force measurement setup with a magnetic manipulation system will precisely control contact force and duration. We will measure pull-off force as a function of contact time (from milliseconds to minutes) on model surfaces (glass, tissue-mimicking hydrogels). Data will be analyzed using viscoelastic contact mechanics models (e.g., extended Maugis-Dugdale or Maxwell/Kelvin-Voigt based frameworks) to quantify time constants and correlate them with material properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative model for time-dependent adhesive failure in soft microrobotic contacts, directly informing the design of perching mechanisms and surface-climbing robots. It will establish guidelines for optimizing contact time and material viscoelasticity to achieve reliable, repeatable adhesion for medical and mobile microrobots.
proposals.novelty: While material creep is studied in bulk adhesion, its explicit, dynamic coupling to the detachment performance of actively controlled, soft microrobotic structures is unexplored. This bridges the gap between macroscopic viscoelastic theory and the operational reality of small-scale robots.

proposals.title: Morphological Optimization of Bio-Inspired Synthetic Seta Arrays for Enhanced Adhesion in Climbing Microrobots
proposals.motivation: Bio-inspired dry adhesives based on gecko-like synthetic setae are crucial for enabling small-scale robots to climb vertical surfaces and perch efficiently, as seen in Metin Sitti's prior work on jumping robots. However, achieving robust and reliable adhesion across diverse real-world surfaces remains a challenge, as performance is highly sensitive to the precise geometry and spatial distribution of the fibrillar structures. A systematic, data-driven optimization of these parameters is needed to move beyond empirical, trial-and-error design and unlock higher, more predictable adhesive forces for advanced microrobotic mobility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an optimal, non-uniform spatial distribution of setae with tailored tip shapes (e.g., spatular, mushroom-like) exists, which maximizes effective contact area and shear adhesion force by mitigating self-matting and uneven stress distribution observed in uniform, densely-packed arrays.
proposals.method: We will fabricate polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) setal arrays using soft lithography, varying tip geometry (spatula diameter, stalk angle), stalk height, and areal density in a controlled design-of-experiments matrix. Adhesive performance (normal pull-off and shear forces) will be characterized on standardized smooth and rough substrates using a micro-force tester. The experimental data will train a Gaussian Process Regression model to map the multi-dimensional design space to adhesive performance, and a Bayesian Optimization algorithm will iteratively propose new, high-performing seta array designs for fabrication and validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a validated, optimized design rule for synthetic dry adhesives, directly applicable to the feet of perching and climbing microrobots. It will provide a generalizable computational-experimental framework for morphology optimization in bio-inspired robotics. The resulting adhesive patches will enable more reliable anchoring for small robots in unstructured environments, advancing towards medical or inspection applications.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored individual seta shapes, the systematic co-optimization of geometry and non-uniform spatial packing density using a closed-loop machine learning framework is novel. This approach moves beyond static, uniform arrays to create functionally graded adhesives tailored for microrobotic dynamic loading conditions.

proposals.title: A Multi-Scale Computational Framework for Predicting Hierarchical Bio-Inspired Adhesion: From Spatula to Whole Toe
proposals.motivation: Bio-inspired dry adhesives, modeled on gecko setae, hold immense promise for climbing robots and medical grippers. However, current design is largely empirical, lacking predictive models that integrate contributions from the nanoscale spatula, microscale seta, and macroscale array. A physics-based, multi-level model is needed to rationally design adhesive systems with predictable and tunable force profiles for specific robotic tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: The total adhesive force of a hierarchical fibrillar structure can be accurately predicted by a computational model that integrates nanoscale van der Waals contact mechanics, microscale fiber bending and buckling, and macroscale load-sharing and peel dynamics.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-scale finite element model using COMSOL Multiphysics. The nanoscale spatula-pad contact will be modeled using a Lennard-Jones potential-based cohesive zone. Arrays of these contact elements will be integrated into Timoshenko beam models for setal bending. Macroscale toe adhesion will be simulated using a statistical representation of setal density and orientation, incorporating anisotropic peel models. The framework will be validated against pre-2026 experimental data from synthetic adhesive patches and Sitti group's gecko-inspired microfiber arrays.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated, predictive computational tool for designing hierarchical dry adhesives, moving the field beyond trial-and-error. It will establish scaling laws linking material properties and fibrillar geometry to overall adhesive performance, directly informing the design of next-generation climbing and perching robots.
proposals.novelty: While individual models exist for single spatula or setal array mechanics, the integration of these scales into a single predictive framework for whole-toe adhesion is novel. This approach uniquely combines contact mechanics, structural mechanics, and statistical load distribution in a way not previously achieved.

proposals.title: Nanoscale Adhesion Mapping of Gecko Spatulae via AFM with Bioinspired Tips for Advanced Microrobotic Feet
proposals.motivation: Gecko-inspired dry adhesives are crucial for climbing robots and medical device grippers, but their performance hinges on nanoscale contact mechanics at the terminal spatula structures. Pre-2026, models rely on averaged measurements, lacking direct spatial mapping of adhesion forces across a single spatula, which limits the rational design of next-generation, high-performance bioinspired adhesives for microrobots operating in complex environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the nanoscale adhesion force of a gecko spatula is not uniform but exhibits a spatially heterogeneous map correlated with surface topography and material composition, and that replicating this map in synthetic structures will significantly enhance the adhesion performance of microrobotic feet.
proposals.method: We will use an Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) in force-volume mode to acquire dense grids of force-distance curves on isolated gecko spatulae. Custom AFM tips will be fabricated via two-photon polymerization to mimic the spatula's spatular shape and material stiffness. Adhesion force maps will be generated by automated analysis of retraction curves using a custom algorithm in Igor Pro or Gwyddion, extracting peak adhesion force per pixel. Correlative SEM imaging will link force maps to nanoscale topography.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative, nanoscale adhesion force map of a biological gecko spatula, providing a ground-truth dataset for bioinspired adhesive design. It will directly inform the fabrication of hierarchical adhesive structures for Sitti lab's climbing and perching microrobots, leading to feet with more predictable and controllable adhesion, especially in dynamic, real-world tasks.
proposals.novelty: While AFM studies of gecko setae exist, mapping adhesion across a *single spatula* with bioinspired custom tips to directly guide synthetic fabrication for microrobotics is novel. This bridges fundamental nanomechanics with applied robotic design in a way prior averaged measurements do not.

proposals.title: Molecular-Level Adhesion Programming in Synthetic Gecko-Inspired Microrollers for Targeted Drug Delivery
proposals.motivation: Gecko-inspired adhesives have enabled macroscale climbing robots, but their miniaturization for medical microrobots is limited by poor understanding of adhesion at the molecular scale. Pre-2026 work on magnetic soft microrobots and microrollers lacks the ability to programmatically control adhesion to specific biological surfaces, which is critical for targeted therapeutic applications in complex fluidic environments like the vasculature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by functionalizing the tips of synthetic gecko-inspired micro-pillars with specific molecular groups (e.g., catechols, thiols) or engineered protein fragments (e.g., fibronectin domains), we can program the adhesion performance of magnetic microrollers to selectively bind to target surfaces under dynamic flow conditions.
proposals.method: We will fabricate magnetic elastomer micropillars using two-photon polymerization, inspired by Sitti's prior work on soft microrobots. Pillar tips will be functionalized via covalent bonding with selected molecular groups. Adhesion performance will be quantified using a custom micro-tribometer under controlled humidity and in fluid flow chambers. We will employ high-speed microscopy and traction force microscopy to correlate detachment forces with molecular interface chemistry. Machine learning (e.g., Random Forest regression) will analyze the multi-parametric data (chemical group, pillar geometry, flow rate) to predict adhesion outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first design rules for molecularly programmable adhesion in synthetic gecko-inspired microrobots. It will contribute a new class of 'smart' microrollers whose adhesion can be tuned for specific biomedical surfaces, directly advancing towards targeted drug delivery and intraocular theranostics. The dataset will bridge molecular chemistry and robotic locomotion performance.
proposals.novelty: While molecular adhesion is studied in biomaterials and gecko adhesion is used in macroscale robots, the deliberate programming of molecular interfaces to control the locomotion and targeting performance of magnetic soft microrobots is a novel, bio-inspired control strategy. This moves beyond physical morphology to chemical functionality for robotic control.

proposals.title: Magnetically Actuated Soft Adhesive Pads with Dynamic Surface Morphology for Adaptive Locomotion on Contaminated Surfaces
proposals.motivation: Soft adhesive pads enable robots to climb walls and manipulate delicate objects, but their performance degrades drastically on wet, dusty, or rough surfaces, limiting real-world deployment. Existing solutions often use static microstructures or passive materials, which cannot adapt to changing conditions or clear debris. A dynamic adhesive system that actively reconfigures its surface topography in response to environmental feedback is needed for robust, condition-adaptive adhesion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a soft adhesive pad with a sub-surface array of magnetic actuators, controlled by a simple contact-quality feedback loop, can dynamically generate optimal surface features (e.g., wrinkles for conformal contact, channels for debris clearance) to maintain strong, pressure-modulated adhesion across diverse and contaminated surfaces.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a soft elastomeric composite pad with embedded, magnetically responsive microparticles arranged in a grid. An external, programmable magnetic field will actuate these regions to create localized surface wrinkles or channels. A real-time feedback loop using a simple optical or impedance-based sensor will monitor contact integrity and trigger specific morphing patterns via a pre-trained classifier. We will benchmark performance against static gecko-inspired and mushroom-shaped adhesives on surfaces with controlled contamination (dust, moisture).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first prototype of a condition-adaptive adhesive pad that actively morphs its topography, significantly extending the operational envelope of climbing and perching robots. It will establish a design and control framework for dynamic surface morphing in soft adhesives, contributing a new strategy for robust bio-inspired attachment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses magnetic fields for robot locomotion or flow control, applying it to dynamically reconfigure the *surface topography of an adhesive pad* for real-time adaptation is novel. This moves beyond static or passively adaptive adhesives to an actively controlled, multi-functional interface.

proposals.title: Magnetically Triggered Phase-Change Adhesive Pads for Untethered Microrobot Perching and Drug Release
proposals.motivation: Untethered microrobots for targeted drug delivery require reliable on-demand adhesion to biological surfaces, such as vascular walls or tissues, to perform localized therapy. Current reversible adhesives for soft robots often rely on complex pressure modulation or electroadhesion, which are challenging to implement in small-scale, wirelessly controlled systems within confined, fluid-filled environments. A simple, solid-state switching mechanism for adhesion strength would enable new capabilities in medical microrobotics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embedding magnetically responsive shape memory polymer (SMP) microstructures within a soft polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) adhesive matrix will create a composite whose adhesion strength can be reversibly switched via remote magnetic heating, enabling controlled attachment and detachment of untethered microrobots without applied pressure.
proposals.method: We will fabricate composite pads by dispersing ferromagnetic nanoparticles (e.g., Fe3O4) into a thermoplastic polyurethane SMP with a tailored glass transition temperature (Tg ~ 40-45°C). This composite will be structured into micropillar arrays using soft lithography to enhance gecko-inspired conformal contact. An integrated, wireless microroller platform will be used for testing. Adhesion strength will be characterized via a custom micro-force setup while applying alternating magnetic fields (using pre-2026 electromagnetic coil systems) to inductively heat the SMP above Tg, causing softening and detachment.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for reversible adhesion in microrobotics, where adhesion is controlled by modulating the material's modulus rather than contact pressure. It will yield a functional material and a wireless actuation strategy directly applicable to in vivo perching and drug release scenarios, advancing towards theranostic applications. The project will also provide fundamental insights into the interplay between viscoelastic properties, microstructure, and adhesive performance under thermal cycling.
proposals.novelty: While SMPs are known, their integration as the active element for *reversible dry adhesion* in untethered microrobots, triggered by wireless magnetic heating, is novel. This approach bypasses the need for complex peeling mechanisms or applied normal force modulation, which is a significant constraint at the microscale.

proposals.title: Reconfigurable Heterogeneous Microsystems via Magnetically-Triggered Transfer Printing with Phase-Change Adhesives
proposals.motivation: Current heterogeneous integration and transfer printing techniques create static assemblies, limiting the adaptability and repairability of complex microsystems like wearable biosensors or soft robotic skins. There is a critical need for manufacturing paradigms that allow for post-deployment reconfiguration, functional upgrades, or in-situ repair without damaging delicate components or the substrate. This is especially relevant for biomedical and field-deployable devices where replacement is costly or impossible.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating stimuli-responsive, reversible adhesives based on low-melting-point alloys or paraffin wax composites into a magnetic transfer printing process will enable the selective, non-destructive release and re-adhesion of specific microcomponents, allowing for the field-programmable reconfiguration of integrated systems.
proposals.method: We will develop soft elastomeric stamps with embedded microheaters and ferromagnetic particles for magnetic control and locomotion. The stamp's tip will be coated with a composite adhesive (e.g., Field's metal or wax doped with magnetic nanoparticles) whose stiffness and adhesion can be switched via localized Joule heating from the microheater. A closed-loop control system, using vision feedback, will coordinate the stamp's magnetic manipulation (inspired by microroller locomotion) with thermal triggering to perform pick-and-place operations. This will be validated by dynamically reconfiguring a model system comprising heterogeneous chips (sensors, LEDs) on a flexible substrate.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new platform for creating dynamically reconfigurable hybrid microsystems, moving beyond static assembly. It will provide a foundational methodology for in-field repair and upgrading of complex devices, directly contributing to the development of sustainable and fault-tolerant electronics. The integration of stimuli-responsive adhesion with robotic manipulation bridges materials science and microrobotics.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of reversible, stimuli-responsive adhesives with magnetically actuated soft transfer printing for *in-situ* reconfiguration is novel. Unlike existing transfer printing, which is largely irreversible, our approach enables programmable disassembly and reassembly, creating devices with a dynamic lifespan.

proposals.title: Electro-Thermally Modulated Gecko-Inspired Adhesives for Untethered Microrobot Perching and Drug Delivery
proposals.motivation: Untethered microrobots for targeted drug delivery require the ability to adhere to biological surfaces without continuous locomotion, enabling sustained local therapeutic action. Existing gecko-inspired adhesives for climbing robots often rely on mechanical pressure modulation for detachment, which is challenging to implement efficiently at the microscale and in confined, fluidic environments like blood vessels. A reversible adhesion mechanism controlled by non-mechanical stimuli is therefore a critical unmet need for in vivo medical microrobotics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite adhesive pad, integrating a gecko-inspired microfibrillar structure with a thermally responsive polymer and a patterned Joule-heating element, will enable rapid, reversible adhesion strength modulation in response to applied electric fields, without requiring changes in applied normal pressure.
proposals.method: We will fabricate mushroom-shaped polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) micro-pillars doped with thermoresponsive poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) microgels. A soft magnetic composite base layer will enable robot steering via external magnetic fields. Sputter-deposited, serpentine-shaped gold traces on the pillar sidewalls will serve as Joule heaters. Adhesion strength will be characterized on synthetic and ex vivo tissue surfaces under varying electric field parameters (frequency, voltage) using a micro-force tester. The system will be integrated onto magnetic 'microrollers' to demonstrate on-demand perching and release in fluid flow channels.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new materials-based architecture for active adhesion control in soft microrobots, directly applicable to intravascular perching for localized drug elution. It will establish a quantitative framework linking thermal stimulus, polymer phase transition, and dry/wet adhesion performance. The resulting platform advances towards biomedical microrobots capable of complex sequences of locomotion, adhesion, and release.
proposals.novelty: While gecko adhesives and stimuli-responsive polymers are well-studied, their integration into a magnetically steerable, electrically activated composite for microscale, pressure-independent adhesion switching in fluidic environments is novel. This approach moves beyond macroscopic climbing robots and simple thermal molding, enabling precise, in-situ adhesion tuning for medical microrobots.

proposals.title: Reversible Adhesion for Microrobotic Perching and Drug Delivery Using Stiff and Viscoelastic Micro-Stamps
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work in bio-inspired reversible adhesion for climbing and perching robots heavily relies on soft elastomers, which can degrade under chemical exposure and have limited load capacity. For medical microrobots, such as those for intraocular drug delivery, a robust, chemically resistant adhesive mechanism is needed to enable stable attachment to wet, dynamic tissue surfaces without leaving residue or causing damage upon detachment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that micro-patterned stamps made from stiff polymers or viscoelastic materials can achieve reversible, strong adhesion through controlled surface contact and energy dissipation, outperforming traditional soft elastomers in wet environments and under shear loads.
proposals.method: We will fabricate micro-patterned stamps (e.g., pillar arrays) from stiff (e.g., SU-8) and viscoelastic (e.g., PDMS with tailored cross-linking) materials. Adhesion performance (pull-off force, shear resistance) will be characterized on dry, wet, and tissue-mimetic surfaces using a micro-force sensor. A magnetic actuation platform, inspired by Sitti's microroller and microswimmer work, will be integrated to demonstrate controlled attachment, rolling locomotion, and detachment of a sub-millimeter robot in a fluidic environment.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new material paradigm for reversible dry/wet adhesion in microrobotics, directly applicable to targeted drug delivery and in-situ sensing. It will provide a quantitative framework for designing adhesion based on material stiffness and viscoelasticity rather than compliance alone.
proposals.novelty: The approach deliberately moves away from the dominant elastomer-based gecko adhesion model, instead exploiting the controlled interfacial mechanics of stiff and viscoelastic materials for reversible bonding in biomedical microrobotics, a largely unexplored direction.

proposals.title: Programmable Acoustic Holograms for Non-Contact Microrobotic Assembly and Sorting
proposals.motivation: Current microrobotic assembly and targeted drug delivery often rely on contact-based manipulation (e.g., magnetic grippers, microrollers) or optical tweezers, which are ineffective for optically transparent or sensitive materials. A non-contact, versatile method for manipulating a wide range of micro-objects, irrespective of their optical or magnetic properties, is critically needed for advanced biomedical applications and micro-fabrication. This project addresses this gap by developing an acoustic analog to optical trapping.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamic acoustic holograms, generated by programmable ultrasonic transducer arrays, can create precise 3D pressure fields capable of exerting controlled radiation forces to trap, sort, and assemble micro-scale objects in a liquid medium without physical contact.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a 2D array of high-frequency piezoelectric transducers. Using pre-2026 iterative phase-retrieval algorithms (e.g., Gerchberg-Saxton) and acoustic field synthesis techniques, we will program the array to generate dynamic holographic pressure patterns. These patterns will create stable acoustic traps and transport pathways in a microfluidic chamber. We will test the system's performance by sorting drug-loaded microcapsules and assembling soft polymer micro-components, quantifying trapping force, speed, and spatial resolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new platform for contactless, selective micromanipulation and assembly, directly applicable to organizing therapeutic carriers or micro-robots in biologically relevant environments. It will contribute a novel tool to the fields of microrobotics and targeted drug delivery by enabling the handling of materials where optical or magnetic methods fail.
proposals.novelty: While acoustic tweezers exist, the use of dynamically programmable holograms from transducer arrays for deterministic, parallel assembly and sorting of micro-objects represents a significant advance over static or single-beam systems, merging concepts from wavefront shaping with microrobotic task execution.

proposals.title: Deterministic Micro-Assembly via Programmable Acoustic and Magnetic Fields for Untethered Microrobot Construction
proposals.motivation: Precise assembly of micro-scale components is critical for advanced micro-robotics and biomedical devices, yet contact-based methods risk contamination and damage. Non-contact methods like optical tweezers have limited force and material compatibility. A robust, scalable, and biocompatible non-contact assembly strategy is needed to build complex microstructures from diverse components, such as drug-loaded particles and soft actuators, for applications in targeted drug delivery and microfactory assembly lines.
proposals.hypothesis: That a hybrid field approach, combining dynamically steerable acoustic pressure nodes for coarse positioning and localized magnetic gradients for fine alignment and bonding, can achieve deterministic, contact-free assembly of micro-objects with different material properties.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-physics platform integrating a high-frequency ultrasonic transducer array (for generating reconfigurable acoustic traps) with an electromagnetic coil system (for applying precise magnetic torques and gradients). Target objects will be functionalized: soft, drug-loaded covalent organic framework (COF) particles will be diamagnetic, while complementary 'gripper' microrollers will be impregnated with magnetic nanoparticles. A hierarchical control algorithm will first use acoustic levitation to position components, then employ model-predictive control of magnetic fields to orient and snap them together via capillary or specific chemical interactions, monitored via high-speed microscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new methodology for heterogeneous micro-assembly without physical contact, directly applicable to constructing hybrid microrobots for theranostics. It will provide a validated control framework for multi-physical field manipulation and demonstrate the assembly of functional micro-devices, such as a light-driven microswimmer with a magnetically steerable component.
proposals.novelty: While acoustic and magnetic manipulation exist separately, their synergistic, hierarchical integration for sequential pick-and-place operations on a micro-scale assembly line is novel. This moves beyond simple particle trapping to the programmable construction of complex, multi-material microsystems.

proposals.title: Protein-Folding-Inspired Hierarchical Assembly of 3D Heterogeneous Microsystems via Magnetic Transfer Printing
proposals.motivation: Printed electronics and microsystems are limited by the direct, monolithic fabrication of complex 3D devices, which forces compromises in materials and performance. Inspired by structural biology, where a 1D polypeptide chain folds into a functional 3D protein through hierarchical steps, we propose a new paradigm for microsystem integration. This decouples the optimal fabrication of sub-components from their final 3D assembly, enabling more sophisticated and high-performance devices for applications like soft microrobotics and implantable medical devices.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical assembly protocol, where 2D-printed functional elements are first pre-folded into freestanding 3D modules (secondary/tertiary structure) and then integrated onto a system substrate via designed interfacial materials (quaternary structure), will yield printed electronic systems with greater complexity and performance than direct printing allows.
proposals.method: We will develop a magnetic transfer printing methodology leveraging Metin Sitti's expertise in magnetic actuation and soft robotics. First, 2D silicon membranes (logic), liquid metal traces (interconnects), and carbon-based films (micro-supercapacitors) will be printed on sacrificial layers. Magnetic micro-rollers or soft magnetic elastomer actuators will then perform controlled folding, rolling, and buckling to form 3D modules. Finally, these modules will be integrated onto a soft substrate using a final transfer step with conductive, pressure-sensitive adhesives or sinterable nanoparticle inks to establish electrical and mechanical bonds.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new hierarchical design and assembly framework for printed electronics, directly inspired by biological principles. It will demonstrate a functional, multi-material microsystem (e.g., a simple autonomous micro-robot or sensor node) with decoupled power, logic, and sensing modules. The protocol will provide a scalable pathway to complex 3D microsystems not achievable by current additive manufacturing.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly mapping the hierarchical folding paradigm of structural biology (primary to quaternary structure) to a deterministic micro-assembly process, and in using magnetic soft robotic tools for the precise manipulation and integration of pre-assembled, heterogeneous functional modules.

proposals.title: Magnetically Guided Assembly of Multi-Material Microsystems via Deterministic Transfer Printing for Untethered Biomedical Microrobots
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, creating functional, untethered microrobots for biomedical applications like targeted drug delivery requires integrating disparate components—soft actuators, rigid sensors, and electronic circuits—into a single, sub-millimeter system. Conventional microfabrication struggles with such heterogeneous, 3D integration on non-planar surfaces. Transfer printing offers a promising assembly method, but its application to complex, free-moving microrobotic systems remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequential, magnetically guided deterministic transfer printing process can reliably assemble multi-material microcomponents (e.g., light-emitting diodes, temperature sensors, and drug-loaded polymer matrices) onto a soft magnetic mobile platform to create a fully functional, wirelessly controlled theranostic microrobot.
proposals.method: We will fabricate donor wafers with releasable microcomponents (InGaN micro-LEDs, Pt resistance temperature detectors, and biodegradable drug reservoirs) using standard lithography. A soft magnetic microroller platform, inspired by Sitti's previous work, will serve as the mobile receiver. Using an automated micro-manipulator with a viscoelastic elastomer stamp, components will be sequentially picked and placed onto the microroller. Magnetic fields will precisely orient the roller for printing onto its curved surface. Final system functionality will be tested by demonstrating magnetic locomotion, light-triggered drug release, and localized temperature sensing in a simulated physiological flow.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a complete pipeline for the 3D heterogeneous integration of electronic and functional materials onto mobile microrobotic platforms. It will provide a concrete methodology for building sophisticated, multi-task medical microrobots, moving beyond single-material or simple geometric designs. The resulting prototype will serve as a benchmark for complexity in untethered microsystem assembly.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging deterministic transfer printing—typically used for static, planar electronics—with magnetically actuated soft mobile robots to create wirelessly functional 3D microsystems. This approach is distinct from monolithic fabrication or stochastic self-assembly, enabling precise, customized integration of disparate components for advanced in-situ biomedical operation.

proposals.title: Closed-Loop Adaptive Microstamping with Magnetically Reconfigurable Surfaces for Fidelity Control in Soft Material Patterning
proposals.motivation: Microstructuring of soft materials like hydrogels and polymers is crucial for biomedical microrobots, drug delivery scaffolds, and cell culture substrates. However, static stamping techniques fail to account for dynamic material behaviors such as viscoelastic relaxation, flow inhomogeneity, and local pressure variations, leading to defective patterns. An adaptive system that compensates in real-time is needed to ensure high-fidelity microstructures across diverse and variable materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a closed-loop stamping system, using a magnetically actuated reconfigurable surface and real-time sensor feedback, can dynamically correct for material inhomogeneities and process variations to maintain microstructural fidelity across polymers, metals, and soft hydrogels.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a soft, magnetically actuated stamp surface composed of an elastomer embedded with neodymium-iron-boron microparticles. Local pressure and material displacement will be monitored via integrated piezoresistive and optical flow sensors. A model predictive control (MPC) algorithm will process this sensor data to dynamically adjust the stamp's local geometry by applying targeted magnetic fields via an array of programmable electromagnetic coils, enabling real-time compensation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first closed-loop adaptive microstamping platform, delivering a new methodology for high-fidelity patterning of heterogeneous and soft materials. It will generate specific adaptive correction protocols for key material classes (polymers, metals, hydrogels), directly advancing the fabrication of complex biomedical microrobots and functional meta-surfaces.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond static or open-loop programmable stamps by integrating real-time sensor feedback for dynamic, in-process correction. The use of a magnetically soft composite for the reconfigurable surface, combined with a model-based control strategy for material compensation, is a novel fusion of Sitti's expertise in magnetic actuation and soft robotics with precision manufacturing.

proposals.title: Material-Class-Tailored Microstructured Stamps for Multifunctional Soft Microrobotic Surfaces
proposals.motivation: The fabrication of functional micro- and nanoscale surface patterns on soft robots and medical devices is crucial for enhancing locomotion, adhesion, and drug-loading capabilities. Current microstructuring methods, such as replica molding, often use generic stamp geometries that do not account for the distinct viscoelastic, curing, or wetting properties of target materials like soft polymers, liquid metals, or biological hydrogels, limiting pattern fidelity and functional performance. Tailoring stamp geometry to material class is a critical, unexplored step towards optimizing surface-driven functions in microrobotics and biomedical interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically optimizing the feature geometry (e.g., pillar aspect ratio, undercut profile, cavity taper) of elastomeric stamps for specific material classes (soft polymers, metals, bio-gels), we can significantly improve the transfer fidelity, yield, and functional performance of the resulting microstructured surfaces in applications like adhesion and flow control.
proposals.method: We will employ a computational design-of-experiments (DOE) approach, using finite element analysis (FEA) in COMSOL to model demolding stresses and material flow for different stamp geometries and material constitutive models. High-resolution 3D printing (e.g., two-photon polymerization) will fabricate master molds for PDMS stamps with parametrically varied features. We will test stamp performance by replicating structures in (1) UV-curable polymers (e.g., NOA), (2) low-melting-point alloys (e.g., Field's metal), and (3) photocrosslinkable hydrogels (e.g., GelMA). Success will be quantified by atomic force microscopy (AFM) for fidelity and by functional tests: adhesion force measurements for polymers and flow resistance assays in microfluidic channels for drug-loaded hydrogel patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first set of design rules linking stamp geometry to optimal patterning outcomes for major material classes used in small-scale robotics and biomedicine. It will directly enable higher-performance adhesive feet for perching microrobots, more reliable liquid metal circuit patterning for soft actuators, and improved drug-eluting microtextures for therapeutic devices, advancing the field of tailored surface fabrication.
proposals.novelty: While microstructuring is common, the systematic optimization of stamp geometry *specifically for the material's mechanical class*—rather than for a single material—is a novel design paradigm. This approach moves beyond trial-and-error to a principled framework, integrating Sitti lab's expertise in soft robotics, magnetic actuation, and biomedical applications.

proposals.title: Optically Programmable Shape Memory Polymer Stamps for Spatially Selective Microtransfer Printing
proposals.motivation: Transfer printing of micro-scale devices and tissues requires precise, dynamic control of stamp adhesion to handle fragile, heterogeneous materials. Existing methods using thermal or mechanical actuation lack spatial selectivity and speed. A non-contact method for rapid, localized modulation of stamp geometry and contact would enable high-yield, multi-material assembly for flexible electronics and bio-integrated devices.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating photothermal dopants (e.g., gold nanorods or carbon black) into a shape memory polymer (SMP) matrix will enable optically triggered, spatially selective shape recovery, allowing real-time control of stamp topography and adhesion for programmable microtransfer printing.
proposals.method: We will fabricate SMP (e.g., polyurethane-based) stamps doped with photothermal nanoparticles. A digital micromirror device (DMD) will project patterned near-infrared light to locally heat the stamp above its glass transition temperature, triggering programmed shape recovery. Adhesion performance will be quantified using a microforce sensor while transferring silicon microchips and hydrogel microtissues. The system's response time, spatial resolution, and repeatability will be characterized.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for non-contact, active stamp control in transfer printing, directly applicable to assembling complex microsystems. It will provide a foundational framework for optically addressable soft robotic surfaces, extending Metin Sitti's work on magnetic and flow-controlled microrollers to a new actuation modality for manipulation.
proposals.novelty: While photothermal therapy and light-activated SMPs are known, their integration into a functional, optically programmable stamp for real-time, spatially selective adhesion control in micro-assembly is unprecedented. This merges therapeutic nanoparticle concepts with soft robotic manipulation in a novel way.

proposals.title: Parallel Multi-Material Micro-Transfer Molding for High-Throughput Fabrication of Soft Microrobotic Systems
proposals.motivation: The development of advanced functional microrobots and soft micro-devices for targeted drug delivery and surface locomotion requires the integration of multiple materials (e.g., magnetic composites, stimuli-responsive polymers, drugs) into complex 2D/3D microstructures. Existing micro-transfer molding techniques are limited by sequential, single-material deposition, creating a critical throughput bottleneck that hinders the scalable manufacturing of heterogeneous microrobotic systems for biomedical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that decoupling the slow, sequential mold-filling process from the rapid placement step via a coordinated reservoir stamping mechanism will enable the parallel, single-cycle transfer of multiple material inks, dramatically increasing fabrication throughput while maintaining spatial and compositional precision.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a structured polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) stamp with an array of micro-molds, complemented by a donor substrate with a matching array of micro-reservoirs pre-filled with different functional inks (e.g., magnetic nanoparticle-doped elastomers, drug-loaded hydrogels). Using a precision alignment stage, the stamp will be brought into contact with the reservoirs, allowing simultaneous capillary filling of all molds. The loaded stamp will then be reversibly bonded to a target substrate via a soft robotic actuator for controlled release, enabling the parallel patterning of complex material combinations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new high-throughput micro-fabrication platform specifically for soft microrobotics, enabling the rapid prototyping of multi-material devices. It will directly contribute to the field by allowing the scalable manufacturing of microrollers, microswimmers, and drug-delivery capsules with integrated locomotion and therapeutic functions, accelerating their transition to in vitro and in vivo testing.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the coordinated, parallel filling of an elastomeric stamp from a pre-patterned donor substrate, a significant departure from existing sequential dip-pen or inkjet-based methods, to achieve single-cycle, multi-material micro-patterning specifically tailored for microrobotic component integration.

proposals.title: Active Capillary Filling for Microfabrication Using Reversibly Adhesive Stamps and Bio-Inspired Negative Pressure Control
proposals.motivation: Micro-transfer molding struggles with reliable, void-free filling of high-aspect-ratio and complex microfluidic or microrobot templates, limiting the fabrication of advanced soft micro-devices. Pre-2026, bio-inspired fluid transport principles, like xylem function, offer untapped potential for active, controlled micro-scale filling. This work addresses a key bottleneck in manufacturing for soft robotics and targeted drug delivery systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a reversibly adhesive elastomeric stamp with an actively controlled negative pressure system, inspired by plant xylem transport, will enable complete, uniform, and rapid capillary filling of complex micro-cavities with polymer precursors, surpassing passive capillary action.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a soft polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) stamp with micro-cavities and coat it with a thin, pH-sensitive polymer layer for reversible adhesion. This stamp will be sealed against a substrate. A microfluidic pressure controller will apply a precise, spatially uniform negative pressure (0.1-10 kPa) to the stamp's outlet, actively pulling a UV-curable resin into the cavities. The filling dynamics will be modeled using modified Lucas-Washburn equations accounting for active pressure and adhesion. Performance will be quantified by filling completeness (via SEM) and feature fidelity for high-aspect-ratio pillars and channels.
proposals.contribution: This will establish a new active micro-molding technique that reliably produces complex 3D microstructures for soft microrobots and microfluidic devices. It contributes a quantitative bio-inspired fluid control framework to micro-manufacturing, directly benefiting fields requiring intricate polymer components, such as untethered medical robots and organ-on-a-chip systems.
proposals.novelty: The method uniquely merges reversible adhesion for clean demolding with an actively driven, plant-inspired capillary system, moving beyond passive wicking or external positive pressure, which are prone to defects in complex geometries.

proposals.title: Magnetically Actuated Microgrippers for Deterministic Nanoscale Transfer Printing of Single Nanoparticles
proposals.motivation: Precise assembly of single nanoparticles or molecules onto specific substrates is a grand challenge in nanofabrication and nanomedicine, enabling next-generation single-molecule sensors, quantum devices, and targeted drug delivery systems. Existing microtransfer printing techniques lack the spatial resolution and deterministic control for single nanoscale objects, while optical tweezers struggle with non-transparent or complex environments. A versatile, high-throughput method for single-particle transfer is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a soft, magnetically actuated microgripper, fabricated via two-photon lithography and coated with a stimuli-responsive polymer, can achieve deterministic pick-and-place of individual nanoparticles via a combination of magnetic locomotion and controlled adhesion switching.
proposals.method: We will design and 3D-print arrays of microgrippers (5-10 µm) with thermally responsive poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (pNIPAM) tips. An external magnetic field will provide both wireless locomotion (inspired by microroller and magnetic robot work) and actuation to open/close the grippers. The pNIPAM coating will switch adhesion via a localized temperature change (from a micro-laser or Joule heating), enabling pick-up at 25°C (hydrophilic, adhesive) and release at 40°C (hydrophobic, non-adhesive). High-speed microscopy and computer vision tracking will guide the grippers to pre-localized target nanoparticles.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new platform for the deterministic assembly of nanoscale building blocks, directly contributing to bottom-up nanomanufacturing. It will also provide a novel tool for biological studies, such as placing single drug-loaded nanoparticles onto individual cells, advancing theranostic applications.
proposals.novelty: The integration of magnetic soft microrobotics for locomotion with stimuli-responsive polymer chemistry for single-particle adhesion control is a novel synthesis of Sitti's core competencies, moving beyond ensemble manipulation to true single-object addressability.

proposals.title: Magnetically Programmable Conformal Stamp for the Assembly of Macroscopic Heterogeneous Systems
proposals.motivation: The assembly of centimeter-scale functional blocks into complex, robust macroscopic systems (e.g., for soft robotics or biomedical devices) is hindered by part misalignment, surface roughness, and material heterogeneity. Rigid assembly tools fail to ensure uniform interfacial contact, leading to weak bonds and structural defects. An active, compliant stamping technology is needed to bridge the gap between precise microscale fabrication and reliable macroscale integration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a stamping head composed of a dense array of independently controllable, magnetically soft micro-actuators can achieve active, real-time conformation to complex topographies, enabling uniform pressure distribution and strong, defect-free bonding of centimeter-scale components.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a stamp head featuring a 10x10 array of soft magnetic composite pillars (e.g., PDMS with NdFeB particles). Each pillar's displacement will be controlled via a patterned external electromagnetic coil array. A high-resolution 3D surface scan (using laser profilometry) of the target components will provide real-time feedback to a closed-loop control algorithm (PID-based) that individually adjusts pillar height and force. This system will be validated by assembling pre-fabricated functional blocks (e.g., soft robotic segments with embedded fluidic channels) using a UV-curable adhesive.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel programmable assembly platform that actively compensates for geometric imperfections at the macroscale. It will demonstrate a scalable pathway to create robust, multi-material systems from pre-fabricated modules, advancing manufacturing for soft robotics and hybrid devices. The integration of magnetic soft actuator arrays with real-time surface feedback constitutes a significant methodological advance.
proposals.novelty: While conformable stamps exist, the use of a dense, independently controlled array of magnetically soft micro-actuators for *active*, real-time conformation to macroscopic parts based on live topology feedback is a non-obvious and unexplored approach to macroscale assembly, distinct from passive elastomeric stamps or rigid robotic grippers.

proposals.title: Closed-Loop Spatially Adaptive De-Adhesion of Thin Films Using Magnetic Microroller Arrays and Real-Time Adhesion Mapping
proposals.motivation: Precision repair in microelectronics and soft composite materials requires residue-free removal of defective layers without damaging delicate substrates. Existing methods (e.g., laser, chemical, or uniform mechanical peeling) fail to account for spatial heterogeneity in interfacial adhesion, leading to incomplete removal, substrate damage, or contamination. A robust, adaptive de-adhesion strategy is critically needed for sustainable device refurbishment and advanced material rework.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a closed-loop system combining real-time, spatially resolved adhesion sensing with an array of magnetically actuated micro-tools can propagate a controlled separation front, enabling complete and clean de-adhesion of heterogeneous interfaces.
proposals.method: We will develop a soft magnetic microroller array, inspired by Sitti's prior work on microrollers and magnetic soft robots, to apply localized shear and normal forces. An integrated optical coherence tomography (OCT) or traction force microscopy system will map interfacial strain during initial probe actuation, creating a real-time adhesion strength map. A model predictive control (MPC) algorithm will then command individual microrollers to apply spatially and temporally tailored magnetic field sequences, propagating a de-adhesion front from identified weak points. Validation will be performed on model systems: delaminating polymer thin films from silicon wafers and hydrogel layers from biological tissue mimics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for non-destructive, precision material removal by closing the loop between sensing and actuation at the micro-scale. It will deliver a validated control framework for spatially adaptive robotic de-adhesion and demonstrate its efficacy on technologically relevant heterogeneous interfaces.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses uniform stimuli for debonding, this approach uniquely integrates real-time, high-resolution adhesion mapping with a distributed magnetic micro-actuator array to achieve intelligent, spatially selective de-adhesion, directly confronting interfacial heterogeneity.

proposals.title: A Temporally Programmable Shape-Memory Polymer Stamp for Sequential Hierarchical Micro-Assembly of Heterogeneous Components
proposals.motivation: The assembly of complex 3D micro-systems, such as microrobots or drug delivery devices, from heterogeneous components remains a major challenge. Current methods like transfer printing often require multiple, precisely aligned stamps, limiting throughput and complexity. A single, dynamically reconfigurable tool that can sequentially handle components at different scales would revolutionize micro-manufacturing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single shape-memory polymer (SMP) stamp with independently tunable macro-scale curvature and micro/nano-scale surface relief can be thermally programmed to sequentially pick, place, and assemble multi-scale components in a single, aligned process without external tool changes.
proposals.method: We will fabricate SMP (e.g., polyurethane) stamps with embedded magnetic nanoparticles for inductive heating. The stamp will possess a pre-programmed, flat macro-geometry for initial large-component pickup and a micro-patterned surface. Using localized, controlled thermal actuation via magnetic fields, we will first trigger a macro-scale curvature change to engage with smaller components, then modulate the micro-pattern's adhesion via glass transition to perform precise placement. A final cooling cycle will trigger release of the complete assembly.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel dynamic assembly tool that reduces alignment complexity and tool count for micro-assembly. It will demonstrate a proof-of-concept hierarchical assembly of a functional microsystem, such as a microroller with integrated therapeutic payloads, advancing towards automated manufacturing of complex microrobots.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the independent, sequential temporal programming of dual-scale stamp geometries (macro and micro) within a single tool, moving beyond static stamps or those with only one programmable dimension, enabling previously impossible single-tool, multi-step assembly sequences.

proposals.title: Optothermally Programmable Micro-Interfaces for Spatially Selective Cellular Manipulation Using Biofunctionalized pNIPAM Brushes
proposals.motivation: Current methods for manipulating single cells or subcellular structures, such as micropipettes or optical tweezers, often apply forces that can damage delicate membranes or lack spatial programmability. A non-destructive, dynamically reconfigurable interface for cell handling is crucial for advancing single-cell analysis, tissue engineering, and fundamental mechanobiology studies. Existing thermoresponsive surfaces lack the spatial and temporal precision needed for such applications, as they typically rely on global heating.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a biofunctionalized, thermoresponsive poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (pNIPAM) brush interface with localized, low-power optothermal heating will enable the reversible, spatially precise switching of cell adhesion with forces below the threshold for membrane damage.
proposals.method: We will fabricate pNIPAM brushes on transparent substrates and functionalize them with RGD peptides to promote integrin-mediated cell adhesion. Localized heating will be achieved using a focused 980 nm laser diode or a patterned, microfluidic-integrated waveguide system, creating sub-100 µm thermal spots. The adhesion dynamics and the exerted detachment forces will be quantified using traction force microscopy and compared to established thresholds for membrane poration. The system's performance will be validated by patterning, selectively releasing, and gently peeling adherent mammalian cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new platform for non-invasive, programmable cellular manipulation, directly applicable to assembling synthetic tissues or isolating rare cells. It will provide quantitative data on the relationship between optothermal stimulus, polymer phase transition, and generated cellular detachment forces, informing the design of future bio-interfaces.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines the spatial precision of optothermal actuation—a method used in Sitti's group for microrobot control—with the gentle, force-limited action of polymer brush phase transition, creating a decoupled, non-chemical switch for adhesion that is absent in prior global thermal or magnetic methods.

proposals.title: Light-Driven, Reversible Microrobotic Perching via Photoswitchable Molecular Anchors for Single-Cell Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Existing microrobots and soft robotic grippers for biological manipulation rely on magnetic, thermal, or mechanical actuation, which can apply damaging shear or compressive forces to fragile cells and tissues. A non-invasive, spatiotemporally precise method for gentle capture and release is critically needed for applications like single-cell analysis and targeted drug delivery. Current bio-adhesion strategies lack the reversibility and molecular-level control required for such delicate operations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that functionalizing microrobotic surfaces with photoresponsive molecular anchors (e.g., azobenzene-spacer-RGD peptide conjugates) will enable reversible, light-triggered adhesion to cell surfaces. The effective presentation and binding affinity of the adhesive ligands will be controlled by toggling the anchor conformation between trans (extended, adhesive) and cis (retracted, non-adhesive) states using specific wavelengths of light.
proposals.method: We will synthesize azobenzene-based molecular anchors with a polyethylene glycol spacer and a terminal cyclic RGD peptide, a known integrin-binding ligand. These will be covalently grafted onto the surface of magnetic soft composite microrollers, building on our group's expertise. Adhesion performance will be quantified using a custom microfluidic force measurement setup, where a microroller is positioned against a target cell or functionalized bead.
