Adhesion will be initiated with 365 nm light (trans state) and released with 450 nm light (cis state). The system's efficacy will be tested for capturing, transporting, and releasing individual mammalian cells in vitro.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for non-contact, force-free adhesion control in microrobotics, directly applicable to gentle biological manipulation. It will contribute a novel tool for spatiotemporally precise single-cell operations, advancing theranostic applications. The integration of molecular photochemistry with mobile microrobots represents a significant step towards intelligent, multifunctional micro-scale systems.
proposals.novelty: While light-driven microswimmers exist, applying photoresponsive molecular switches to dynamically control the *adhesive interface* of a mobile microrobot for reversible perching is novel. This approach moves beyond bulk material actuation to achieve molecular-scale, reversible control of bio-interactions on a robotic platform.

proposals.title: Magnetically Actuated, Compliant Micro-Scaffolds via Soft Lithography for Dynamic 3D Cell Culture and Mechanotransduction Studies
proposals.motivation: Traditional cell culture scaffolds are often static and mechanically passive, failing to replicate the dynamic, compliant microenvironments found in vivo, which are crucial for proper cell function, differentiation, and tissue development. Pre-2026, there is a growing need for advanced in vitro models that can apply controlled, physiologically relevant mechanical stimuli to cells in 3D. Soft robotics offers manufacturing techniques to create such active, compliant microstructures, bridging a gap in tissue engineering and mechanobiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that soft lithography and 3D printing can be used to fabricate magnetically responsive, elastomeric micro-scaffolds with tunable compliance and complex geometries, and that remote magnetic actuation of these scaffolds will induce significant, beneficial changes in attached cell morphology, alignment, and gene expression through controlled mechanical stimulation.
proposals.method: We will employ soft lithography (e.g., replica molding with PDMS) and micro-scale 3D printing (e.g., two-photon polymerization of soft hydrogels) to create 3D scaffold architectures with pore sizes and strut compliances optimized for specific cell types (e.g., fibroblasts, myoblasts). Magnetic responsiveness will be integrated by dispersing ferromagnetic or superparamagnetic nanoparticles (e.g., NdFeB, iron oxide) into the polymer matrix prior to curing. A pre-2026 multi-coil electromagnetic control system, similar to those used for magnetic microroller actuation, will be programmed to apply precise, time-varying magnetic fields to induce scaffold deformation, generating controlled cyclic strain or fluid flow within the 3D culture.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel platform for dynamic 3D cell culture, enabling unprecedented spatiotemporal control over mechanical cues in vitro. It will contribute fundamental insights into cell-scaffold mechanotransduction and provide a new tool for engineering functional tissue constructs and advanced disease models. The integration of soft robotic fabrication with magnetic actuation directly extends the lab's expertise in microrobotics to the domain of biohybrid systems and regenerative medicine.
proposals.novelty: While soft lithography is used for static scaffolds and magnetic systems actuate rigid microrobots, the combination to create remotely actuated, compliant 3D micro-scaffolds with complex, cell-instructive geometries for dynamic mechanostimulation is a novel biohybrid approach. This moves beyond passive scaffolds or 2D stretching substrates to a fully 3D, programmable mechanical microenvironment.

proposals.title: Magnetically Programmable Microsheet Swarms for Targeted Fluidic Transport and Sensing
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, microscale robots show promise for biomedical and environmental tasks, but achieving collective behaviors in large numbers for fluid manipulation remains a challenge. Existing microrollers and swimmers often lack the reconfigurable surface area needed for efficient fluid transport or distributed sensing. A swarm of microscopic sheet robots could bridge this gap, enabling new capabilities in targeted drug delivery or microfluidic diagnostics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fabricating large numbers of magnetically responsive, soft polymeric microsheets will enable their programmable assembly into dynamic swarms capable of collective fluid transport and environmental sensing via coordinated shape deformation and locomotion.
proposals.method: We will fabricate microsheets (10-100 µm) from soft elastomers (e.g., PDMS) embedded with magnetic nanoparticles. Actuation and control will use pre-2026 external magnetic field systems (multi-coil setups) to generate programmable gradients and rotational fields for rolling, folding, and collective translation. Swarm coordination will be achieved via pre-programmed field sequences and local interactions (e.g., capillary forces, magnetic dipolar coupling) to form transient structures for fluid pumping or chemical gradient mapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new platform for magnetically driven microsheet swarms, demonstrating their utility in fluidic manipulation tasks. It will contribute fundamental principles for the collective control of shape-morphing micro-robots, advancing towards applications in minimally invasive medicine and lab-on-a-chip systems.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in combining the high surface-area-to-volume ratio and shape-morphing capability of microsheets with swarm-level magnetic control, moving beyond the locomotion-centric designs of existing microrollers or microswimmers to enable collective fluidic functions.

proposals.title: Covalent and Non-Covalent Interface Engineering in Magnetic Nanomaterial-Elastomer Composites for High-Performance Soft Microrobotic Actuators
proposals.motivation: The performance of soft magnetic robots and stretchable sensors is critically limited by the interface between functional nanomaterials and the polymer matrix. Poor interfacial adhesion leads to filler aggregation, hysteresis, and mechanical failure under cyclic loading, especially in dynamic applications like locomotion and drug delivery. A systematic understanding of how to engineer these interfaces for optimal force transmission, electrical conductivity, and durability is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically combining covalent tethering and dynamic non-covalent interactions at the nanomaterial-polymer interface, we can create composites that simultaneously exhibit superior stretchability, high sensitivity, and robust cyclic performance in magnetic actuation.
proposals.method: We will synthesize magnetic nanoparticles (e.g., Fe3O4) and nanowires with surface functionalities for covalent bonding (e.g., silanes) and non-covalent interactions (e.g., dopamine, hydrogen-bonding moieties). These will be integrated into PDMS and other elastomers. We will systematically vary the ratio and chemistry of these interfacial linkers. Composite performance will be characterized by measuring magneto-mechanical coupling efficiency, electrical conductivity under strain, and fatigue resistance. Actuation performance will be validated using pre-2026 magnetic control systems to drive microrollers and perching mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design framework for interface-engineered nanocomposites, directly translating to more efficient, durable, and sensitive soft microrobots for biomedical tasks. It will provide quantitative structure-property relationships linking specific interfacial chemistry to macroscale robotic performance metrics like locomotion efficiency and sensor stability.
proposals.novelty: While surface functionalization is known, the deliberate co-engineering of covalent (for stability) and dynamic non-covalent (for energy dissipation and self-healing) interactions at the same interface for magnetic soft robot composites is a novel and biomimetic strategy not yet fully explored in microrobotics.

proposals.title: Soft Microfluidic Fabrication of Embedded Liquid Metal Sensors for Proprioception in Magnetic Soft Millirobots
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, magnetic soft millirobots show great promise for targeted drug delivery and minimally invasive procedures, but a critical limitation is their lack of integrated sensing for closed-loop control. Existing methods for embedding sensors often compromise the robot's softness, flexibility, or miniaturization potential. Microfluidics offers a pathway to create compliant, embedded sensors, but its application for real-time proprioceptive feedback in untethered, magnetically actuated robots remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that soft lithography and microfluidic patterning can be used to create robust, stretchable conductive channels of eutectic gallium-indium (EGaIn) within elastomeric matrices, enabling real-time sensing of deformation (bending, stretching) in magnetic soft millirobots without hindering their locomotion or magnetic actuation performance.
proposals.method: We will use soft lithography with PDMS to create microfluidic networks within the body of magnetic soft robots fabricated from silicone elastomers doped with NdFeB particles. A vacuum-assisted filling technique will inject liquid metal (EGaIn) into the channels to form conductive traces. The sensor design will consist of meandering and straight channel geometries to correlate resistance changes with specific deformation modes. These sensors will be integrated into robot designs such as microrollers or crawling sheets, and their electrical response will be characterized against imposed deformations and during magnetically driven locomotion on surfaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a scalable fabrication method for embedding proprioceptive sensors directly into the bodies of soft millirobots. It will demonstrate, for the first time, real-time kinematic feedback in untethered magnetic soft robots, a key step towards autonomous closed-loop control. The resulting sensorized platforms will enable new studies on robot-environment interaction and enhance theranostic capabilities by allowing adaptive behaviors.
proposals.novelty: While microfluidics and liquid metal sensors exist, their integration as proprioceptive elements into *untethered*, magnetically actuated soft robots for in-situ deformation feedback is novel. This approach uniquely addresses the dual challenge of maintaining soft, compliant actuation while adding sensing functionality, moving beyond prior work focused on external sensing or tethered setups.

proposals.title: 3D Bioprinted, Magnetically Actuated Soft Microrobots with Integrated Biofabricated Strain Sensors for Targeted Drug Delivery
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, untethered microrobots show promise for minimally invasive targeted therapies, but real-time monitoring of their locomotion and interaction with tissues remains a challenge. Implantable electronic sensors often provoke immune responses and lack seamless integration. There is a critical need for biocompatible, conformable sensors that can be directly fabricated onto soft, mobile medical robots to enable closed-loop control and performance assessment in vivo.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a conductive bio-ink, formulated with gelatin methacryloyl (GelMA) and carbon nanotubes, can be directly 3D bioprinted onto magnetically actuated soft microrobots to create fully integrated, biocompatible strain sensors. These sensors will reliably transduce microrobot deformation during surface locomotion (e.g., rolling, crawling) into measurable resistance changes, enabling in-situ locomotion monitoring.
proposals.method: We will fabricate magnetically responsive, drug-loaded soft microrobots using a micro-molding technique with a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) and carbonyl iron powder composite. A multi-material extrusion bioprinter will then directly pattern a conductive GelMA-based bio-ink onto the microrobot's surface in a serpentine geometry. The sensor's piezoresistive response will be characterized under controlled magnetic fields in microfluidic channels simulating vascular flow. Sensor data will be correlated with high-speed video tracking of microroller locomotion performance on synthetic and ex vivo biological surfaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel biofabrication pipeline for creating sensorized, soft biomedical microrobots. It will provide the first demonstration of real-time, in-situ strain sensing on a mobile, untethered microrobot, contributing a critical tool for feedback control. The outcomes will advance theranostic applications by merging targeted drug delivery with diagnostic sensing capabilities in a single biocompatible platform.
proposals.novelty: While biofabrication and microrobots exist separately, the direct integration of a 3D bioprinted, living-material-based sensor onto an actively locomoting soft microrobot is novel. This moves beyond static implants or external tracking, creating an inherently biocompatible, self-sensing theranostic agent.

proposals.title: Electrically Actuated Soft Microrollers via Inverse Design of Piezoresistive Nanocomposite Materials
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 soft robotics relies heavily on separate, often bulky, actuator and sensor systems, limiting miniaturization and integration for biomedical applications like targeted drug delivery. Metin Sitti's prior work on magnetic and flow-driven microrollers demonstrates controlled locomotion but lacks embedded, electrically programmable actuation. Developing intrinsic, material-based actuators from existing sensor composites would enable fully soft, wirelessly controlled micro-robots with closed-loop control capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: That the piezoresistive filler networks in stretchable elastomer composites (e.g., carbon black/PDMS), typically used for strain sensing, can be inversely engineered to function as Joule heating elements, inducing controlled, non-uniform thermal expansion for reversible bending actuation when patterned and powered.
proposals.method: We will fabricate bilayer actuators using Sitti group's established soft lithography, integrating a passive elastomer layer with an active layer of optimized carbon nanotube/PDMS composite. The composite's filler concentration and distribution will be tailored via shear-alignment to create anisotropic electrical pathways, enabling directional Joule heating. A low-voltage, high-frequency alternating current (100 Hz–10 kHz) will be applied via integrated liquid metal microelectrodes to generate rapid thermal cycles, producing cyclic bending for locomotion. Actuation performance (bending angle, frequency, force) will be characterized and integrated onto microroller platforms.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new material-centric paradigm for creating monolithic soft micro-actuators, directly from sensor material platforms. It will contribute a design framework for 'inverse material programming' and demonstrate a novel, electrically powered locomotion mechanism for untethered soft microrobots, advancing towards integrated sensing and actuation for theranostic applications.
proposals.novelty: While composites are well-known as sensors, their systematic inverse design as high-frequency, low-voltage electrothermal actuators for soft microrobot locomotion is unexplored. This approach moves beyond adding separate actuators (e.g., magnetic particles) to instead repurpose the core composite material itself as a dual-function element.

proposals.title: Environmentally Programmed Stochastic Assembly of Microrobotic Swarms via Dynamic Magnetic Landscapes
proposals.motivation: Scalable, parallel micro-assembly remains a critical challenge for manufacturing complex microdevices and tissue engineering. Current non-contact methods using magnetic or acoustic fields require precise, real-time control of each element, limiting throughput and scalability. A method that guides inherently stochastic microrobotic populations through engineered environmental cues could enable robust, high-yield assembly without complex individual feedback.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a population of simple, magnetically responsive microrobotic units (e.g., microrollers or colloidal particles), when subjected to a spatiotemporally programmed but globally applied magnetic field landscape, will self-organize via stochastic interactions and non-contact forces to form deterministic, pre-designed 2D and 3D micro-structures with high probability.
proposals.method: We will fabricate soft magnetic microrollers and Janus particles as our stochastic units. A multi-coil electromagnetic system will generate dynamic magnetic field landscapes, creating regions of high magnetic potential energy (barriers) and low energy (traps). We will program these landscapes using sequences inspired by simulated annealing and stochastic optimal control theory, periodically modulating field parameters to jostle and guide particles. Assembly success will be quantified via high-speed microscopy and computer vision tracking of particle positions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for 'environmental steering' in microrobotics, demonstrating deterministic outcomes from stochastic collective dynamics. It will provide a concrete experimental and algorithmic framework for scalable, parallel micro-assembly without individual unit control, directly applicable to building micro-electromechanical systems and biomimetic scaffolds.
proposals.novelty: The approach merges the robustness of stochastic swarm behaviors with the precision of non-contact magnetic manipulation, moving beyond precise closed-loop control of each agent to the programming of the environment itself to probabilistically guide a population—a concept distinct from existing deterministic magnetic micromanipulation or purely diffusive self-assembly.

proposals.title: Magnetically Actuated Origami/Kirigami Metamaterials for Shape-Morphing Soft Microrobots
proposals.motivation: Untethered microrobots for biomedical applications, such as targeted drug delivery, must navigate complex, confined physiological environments. Current magnetic soft microrobots often have fixed shapes, limiting their ability to adapt to varying lumen sizes or perform different locomotion modes. Integrating programmable mechanical metamaterials based on folding could enable a single microrobot to reconfigure its shape on-demand, enhancing navigation and multi-functional task execution.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding magnetic nanoparticles into the hinges of rationally designed origami/kirigami polymer sheets, we hypothesize that external magnetic fields can induce predictable and reversible 3D shape morphing, enabling a single microrobot to switch between rolling, crawling, and grasping configurations for improved performance in constrained spaces.
proposals.method: We will design 2D precursors using kirigami (cutting) and origami (folding) patterns known to yield auxetic or shape-shifting 3D structures. These will be fabricated from soft polymers (e.g., PDMS) doped with neodymium-iron-boron microparticles. A multi-coil electromagnetic system will generate spatiotemporally controlled magnetic fields for actuation. We will develop a dynamic model coupling magnetic torque with the nonlinear mechanics of the folding hinges to predict and control the morphing sequence, validated via high-speed microscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design and actuation framework for magnetically driven, foldable microrobots. It will demonstrate a new class of soft microrobots capable of on-the-fly shape reconfiguration for adaptive locomotion and object manipulation at the microscale, directly contributing to the field of medical microrobotics.
proposals.novelty: While origami structures and magnetic soft robots exist separately, their integration for creating *in-situ* shape-morphing capabilities in untethered microrobots is novel. This approach moves beyond simple bending to achieve complex, pre-programmed 3D reconfiguration via folding, a principle not yet fully exploited in Sitti's pre-2026 work on microrollers and soft magnetic robots.

proposals.title: Magnetically Actuated Covalent Organic Framework Nanorobots for Targeted Mitochondrial Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Intracellular manipulation of specific organelles, like mitochondria, is critical for understanding and treating metabolic and neurodegenerative diseases. Pre-2026, existing micro/nanorobots primarily operate in extracellular environments or perform bulk delivery, lacking the precision for subcellular mechanical tasks. A platform for direct, targeted organelle interaction inside living cells remains an unmet challenge in nanorobotics and biomedicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that nanoscale robots, constructed from biocompatible covalent organic frameworks (COFs) and powered by external magnetic fields, can be navigated to and mechanically interact with specific organelles within a living cell to alter their function.
proposals.method: We will design sub-200 nm rod-shaped nanorobots using two-photon polymerization lithography of magnetic nanoparticle-doped, biodegradable polymers, followed by surface functionalization with a thin, porous COF layer for drug loading. Locomotion and steering will be achieved via pre-2026 rotating magnetic field systems, similar to those used for microrollers but scaled and adapted for low-Reynolds number intracellular fluid. Targeting will use mitochondrial membrane potential-sensitive peptides (e.g., SS-31) conjugated to the COF surface. Organelle manipulation will be tested by loading the COF pores with uncouplers (e.g., FCCP) for triggered release upon magnetic actuation-induced mechanical stress.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of organelle-specific nanorobots, moving beyond drug carriers to become active intracellular manipulators. It will provide a novel tool for cell biology to probe organelle mechanics and function, and lay the groundwork for therapeutic strategies based on direct organelle correction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the programmability and cargo capacity of COF-based microswimmers with the precise, wireless actuation of magnetic microrobotics to achieve *mechanical* manipulation at the organelle level, a significant step beyond existing diffusive drug delivery systems.

proposals.title: Rate-Dependent and Fatigue-Resistant Bioinspired Adhesives for Dynamic Microrobot Locomotion and Perching
proposals.motivation: For small-scale robots performing medical tasks like targeted drug delivery or in-situ diagnostics, reliable dynamic adhesion under physiological conditions is critical for locomotion on tissues and stable perching for operation. Pre-2026 work on gecko-inspired adhesives and magnetic microrollers has largely characterized static or quasi-static adhesion, leaving a gap in understanding how adhesion performs under the varying loading rates and cyclic stresses encountered during real-world robot motion, which limits robust design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by engineering the viscoelastic and microstructural properties of soft, fibrillar adhesives, we can create surfaces whose adhesion strength and detachment energy are tunable with loading rate and exhibit superior resistance to cyclic fatigue, enabling more predictable and reliable microrobot performance.
proposals.method: We will fabricate soft, micropatterned polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) adhesives with varying pillar geometries and stiffnesses. A custom magnetic microrobotic platform, inspired by Sitti's microrollers, will be used to apply precise, programmable normal and shear loads at controlled rates (0.1-100 mm/s) and cycles. Adhesion force and contact area will be measured in-situ using a high-speed camera and a micro-force sensor. We will systematically vary magnetic field parameters to control loading conditions and analyze performance metrics like pull-off force and work of adhesion as functions of rate and cycle number.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental framework for designing dynamic adhesives for small-scale robots, providing quantitative models linking material properties, loading kinetics, and adhesive performance. It will directly inform the design of next-generation medical microrobots for robust tissue anchoring and the development of avian-inspired perching mechanisms for jumping macroscale robots that require repeated, impact-resistant attachment.
proposals.novelty: While static bioadhesion is well-studied, this investigation uniquely focuses on the dynamic adhesion regime critical for moving robots, combining Sitti's expertise in magnetic actuation and soft microrobotics with a systematic study of rate-dependent and fatigue phenomena not yet explored in this integrated context.

proposals.title: Morphing Microrollers: Shape-Adaptive Magnetic Microswimmers for Enhanced Locomotion in Complex Biological Fluids
proposals.motivation: Helical microswimmers are highly effective in Newtonian fluids, but their locomotion efficiency degrades significantly in the non-Newtonian, heterogeneous, and viscoelastic biological fluids (e.g., mucus, vitreous humor) encountered in targeted drug delivery. Pre-2026 work in Sitti's lab and others has shown that rigid helical propulsion is suboptimal in such environments, creating a critical need for architectures that can adapt to local rheology to maintain efficient transport.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that magnetically actuated, soft microrollers with shape-morphing capabilities will achieve superior locomotion performance in non-Newtonian biological fluids compared to static helical or rigid non-helical designs by dynamically adapting their geometry to the local fluidic environment.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate soft magnetic composite microrobots using pre-2026 techniques like template-assisted self-assembly and two-photon polymerization, incorporating stimuli-responsive polymers or liquid metal segments for shape change. Actuation will be via pre-programmed rotating magnetic fields from an electromagnetic coil system. Locomotion performance (speed, efficiency, force) will be quantified in viscoelastic polymer solutions (e.g., polyacrylamide, mucin gels) using high-speed microscopy. We will employ a comparative analysis framework, benchmarking against helical swimmers and rigid microrollers, and use particle tracking velocimetry to analyze flow fields.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a design framework for shape-adaptive magnetic microswimmers, providing quantitative evidence that optimal morphology is environment-dependent. It will deliver a new class of microroller capable of efficient navigation in biologically relevant, non-Newtonian fluids, directly advancing the field of minimally invasive theranostics.
proposals.novelty: While shape-morphing in soft robotics is known, its systematic application to enhance the fundamental locomotion mechanics of magnetically driven microrollers in non-Newtonian fluids is unexplored. This moves beyond comparing static shapes to creating a single, dynamically reconfigurable agent.

proposals.title: Scalable Parallel Microfabrication of Magnetically Actuated Covalent Organic Framework Microswimmers for Clinical-Grade Production
proposals.motivation: Pre-clinical research on microswimmers for targeted drug delivery, as exemplified by Sitti's work on Covalent Organic Framework (COF)-based systems, has shown immense therapeutic promise. However, a critical translational bottleneck is the lack of manufacturing methods capable of producing the vast quantities (billions) of uniform, functional micro-agents required for a single clinical dose. Current serial fabrication techniques like direct laser writing are prohibitively slow and costly for mass production.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a parallel micro-molding process, using a master template fabricated via two-photon polymerization, can reliably produce large batches of magnetically actuated COF-composite microswimmers with the geometric uniformity and magnetic anisotropy necessary for controlled locomotion and drug loading.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a high-fidelity, reusable polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) mold from a 3D-printed master template containing thousands of microswimmer negative cavities. The microswimmer bodies will be formed by filling the mold with a photocurable resin pre-loaded with neodymium-iron-boron (NdFeB) nanoparticles and COF precursors. After UV curing and demolding, a secondary solvothermal step will crystallize the COF network within the polymer matrix to create the porous drug carrier. We will integrate a robotic dispensing system for high-viscosity resin filling and a magnetic alignment stage to orient nanoparticles for anisotropic magnetic response prior to curing.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational manufacturing pipeline for batch production of functional microswimmers, directly addressing the scale-up challenge for biomedical applications. It will yield the first quantitative analysis of batch-to-batch consistency in microswimmer geometry, magnetic moment, and drug loading capacity, providing essential metrics for future Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP) translation.
proposals.novelty: While parallel molding is known in MEMS, its application to create complex, porous, and magnetically anisotropic microswimmers in a batch process is novel. The integration of COF synthesis within a molded polymer scaffold to create a monolithic, multi-functional agent for combined locomotion and drug delivery has not been demonstrated at scale.

proposals.title: Magnetic Soft Microrollers with Onboard Finite-State Machine Control for Adaptive Locomotion in Confined Environments
proposals.motivation: Soft, small-scale magnetic robots, such as microrollers, offer immense potential for minimally invasive biomedical tasks like targeted drug delivery. However, their functionality is currently limited by reliance on external control systems for every motion decision, preventing autonomous adaptation to complex, dynamic environments like blood vessels. Embedding control logic directly onto the robot body is a critical step towards true autonomy at the microscale.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating microscale electronic circuits implementing finite-state machines (FSMs) onto magnetic soft composite bodies, we can create microrobots capable of autonomously switching locomotion gaits (e.g., rolling, tumbling, crawling) in response to simple onboard sensor inputs, enabling navigation through heterogeneous environments without continuous external instruction.
proposals.method: We will fabricate soft magnetic elastomer bodies using NdFeB particles in a PDMS matrix. Onboard control will be implemented via microscale, flexible hybrid electronics (FHE) fabricated using photolithography and transfer printing, containing a low-power microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M0+ core) and a magnetoresistive sensor. Control logic will be programmed as a finite-state machine, where the sensor input (e.g., local magnetic field strength as a proxy for proximity to a surface) triggers a state transition, commanding a change in the externally applied magnetic field program (e.g., rotational axis or frequency) via a predefined wireless signal. The robot's performance will be tested in microfluidic channels with varying geometry and surface texture.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first prototype of a soft microroller with embedded computational control, demonstrating a foundational architecture for autonomous decision-making at sub-millimeter scales. It will provide a new design paradigm for closing the control loop directly on the robot, moving beyond open-loop magnetic actuation and advancing towards intelligent, environment-responsive medical microrobots.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has focused on the material composition and external magnetic control of microrollers, the direct integration of programmable, state-based control logic onto the soft body itself is novel. This approach merges Sitti's expertise in magnetic soft robots with emerging flexible electronics, creating a new class of robots with embodied intelligence.

proposals.title: Magnetic Origami-Kirigami Soft Microactuators for Programmable Surface Locomotion and Drug Release
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, soft microrobots for biomedical applications face a fundamental challenge: achieving complex, programmable 3D shape-morphing and locomotion modes from simple 2D fabrication. Existing magnetic soft actuators often rely on monolithic material gradients, limiting the diversity of motions and functions like on-demand cargo release within a single, small-scale device.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding magnetic nanoparticles into elastomer sheets pre-patterned with origami folds and kirigami cuts, we hypothesize that a single applied magnetic field can trigger multiple, distinct, and complex 3D actuation sequences—enabling advanced locomotion (e.g., rolling, jumping, perching) and controlled mechanical drug release.
proposals.method: We will design 2D elastomer (e.g., PDMS) sheets with integrated NdFeB nanoparticles, patterned using laser cutting (kirigami) and creasing (origami). Specific folding sequences will be programmed via the spatial distribution of magnetic material and the geometric constraints of the cuts and folds. We will employ finite-element modeling (e.g., COMSOL) coupled with magnetic torque analysis to simulate the actuation. Actuation performance will be tested in microfluidic channels and on biological tissue surfaces under programmable rotating magnetic fields generated by a pre-2026 electromagnetic coil system.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new design paradigm for creating multi-functional soft microactuators from 2D precursors. It will demonstrate a library of magnetic origami-kirigami structures capable of complex surface locomotion and mechanical action, directly advancing theranostic applications such as targeted drug delivery and minimally invasive surgery.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the high degree of motion programmability from origami/kirigami with the untethered, biocompatible actuation of magnetic soft materials at the microscale, specifically for creating multi-modal microrobots from a monolithic 2D sheet.

proposals.title: Strong and Reversible Adhesion in Dense Micro-Roller Arrays via Tip-Only Sidewall Contact
proposals.motivation: Dense arrays of micro-robots for targeted drug delivery or surface manipulation require strong, reversible adhesion to navigate complex biological or engineered environments. Existing bio-inspired adhesives often rely on large-area contact or complex fibrillar structures, which are difficult to scale down and integrate into dense arrays without cross-talk and fouling. A mechanism using only the tips of sidewall structures could enable independent control of individual microrollers in an array, crucial for collective tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by engineering the geometry and surface chemistry of sidewall tips in a dense array, strong and reversible van der Waals or capillary adhesion can be achieved through tip-only contact, decoupling adhesion performance from the bulk of the robot body.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate dense arrays of magnetic soft polymer microrollers with vertical sidewalls featuring mushroom-shaped or spatula-like tips. Using magnetic control, we will precisely engage only the tips with a target surface. Adhesion performance (pull-off force, reversibility) will be quantified via a micro-force sensor while varying tip geometry (diameter, aspect ratio), surface energy (via plasma treatment or hydrophobic coatings), and preload. A high-speed camera will monitor contact mechanics to correlate performance with real contact area.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for adhesion in micro-robotic arrays, enabling stronger and more independent locomotion control. It will provide a quantitative framework linking tip geometry, surface energy, and adhesive performance, directly informing the design of next-generation microrollers for biomedical applications like targeted tissue sampling or drug delivery.
proposals.novelty: While sidewall structures are known for gecko-inspired adhesion, the deliberate restriction of adhesive contact exclusively to engineered tips in a dense, active microroller array is unexplored. This approach fundamentally differs from whole-pad or full-sidewall contact strategies, aiming to minimize interference and maximize reusability in arrayed systems.

proposals.title: Magnetically Programmable Multi-Material Microstructures for Reconfigurable Locomotion and Drug Delivery
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 magnetic soft robotics excels with monolithic materials but lacks the functional complexity needed for advanced biomedical tasks like targeted drug delivery. Integrating materials with distinct magnetic and elastic properties into a single, small-scale system could enable sophisticated shape-morphing and multi-modal locomotion (e.g., rolling, crawling, swimming) in complex physiological environments. This is critical for developing next-generation medical microrobots that can adapt their function in real-time.
proposals.hypothesis: By strategically patterning magnetic nanoparticles and tuning the elastic moduli of distinct material domains within a single microstructure, we can encode complex, pre-programmed 3D shape transformations under uniform magnetic fields, leading to adaptive locomotion and targeted cargo release.
proposals.method: We will fabricate multi-material microstructures using sequential projection micro-stereolithography (PµSL) and embedded 3D printing, combining a stiff, highly magnetized polymer composite with a soft, low-modulus elastomer. Magnetic anisotropy will be engineered via particle alignment during printing. We will develop a computational design framework using finite-element analysis (e.g., COMSOL) coupled with magneto-mechanical modeling to predict shape changes. Actuation will be tested using pre-2026 triaxial electromagnetic coil systems to generate rolling, gradient-climbing, and undulatory gaits.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design and fabrication paradigm for multi-material magnetic microrobots, demonstrating a library of programmable shape-morphing modes from a single construct. It will provide a validated modeling framework for predicting the behavior of heterogeneous magnetic soft matter, advancing the field towards sophisticated in vivo theranostic applications.
proposals.novelty: While existing work uses uniform magnetic properties, this proposal uniquely combines spatially varied magnetic responsiveness *and* mechanical stiffness in a single microfabricated entity to achieve complex, field-programmable kinematics, moving beyond the limitations of single-material 'microrollers' or simple bilayer actuators.

ideas.title: Reversible Adhesion via Phase-Change Polymer Actuation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that modulating applied pressure is necessary for controlling reversible adhesion. By drawing an analogy from the field of Shape Memory Alloys (SMAs) in Actuator Design—where reversible, solid-state phase transformations triggered by temperature produce mechanical work—we adapt the core principle to adhesion. The solution is to design a soft adhesive composite material with embedded shape memory polymer (SMP) microstructures. The adhesive's strength is controlled by thermal cycling: at a lower temperature, the SMP is rigid, enabling strong adhesion via mechanical interlocking or conformal contact; upon heating to a specific transition point, the SMP softens, drastically reducing the interfacial modulus and stress for easy, clean detachment. This provides a method for reversible adhesion where the primary control stimulus is temperature, not applied pressure.

ideas.title: Dynamic Surface Morphing for Condition-Adaptive Adhesion
ideas.description: This system overcomes the static limitation of the original idea by employing a soft composite pad with an actively reconfigurable surface topography. An array of sub-surface actuators, controlled by a simple sensor feedback loop monitoring contact quality, can dynamically generate localized surface features such as wrinkles for conformal contact or channels for debris clearance. This allows a single adhesive pad to maintain robust, pressure-modulated adhesion across diverse, dirty, or wet surfaces by morphing its texture in real-time to suit the immediate challenge. After use, the features retract, preserving the benefits of a smooth, non-fouling default state.

ideas.title: Capillary Transfer Molding via Reversible Adhesion and Bio-Inspired Fluid Control
ideas.description: This idea expands micro-transfer molding by integrating an active, bio-inspired capillary filling mechanism. Drawing an analogy from xylem transport in plants, where negative pressure and fluid-wall adhesion enable controlled long-distance capillary flow, the method uses a reversibly adhesive stamp to form a sealed temporary mold. A controlled negative pressure is then applied to actively and uniformly pull a liquid polymer or resin precursor into the micro-cavity network via capillary action, ensuring complete filling of high-aspect-ratio features. After curing, the reversible adhesion allows for clean demolding. This combines the precision of reversible adhesion for pattern definition with an actively managed capillary filling process inspired by plant physiology, improving reliability for complex micro- and nano-scale structures.

ideas.title: Optically Triggered, Reversible Bio-Adhesion Using Photoresponsive Molecular Anchors
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from organic photochemistry—the use of light-responsive molecular switches like azobenzene—to the problem of low-force adhesion modulation for fragile biological objects. In the analogous field, the reversible photoisomerization of molecules provides a clean, non-invasive method to control material properties. The adapted research idea proposes to functionalize substrates with photoresponsive bio-adhesive anchors. By toggling these anchors between conformational states with specific light wavelengths, the effective presentation of cell-binding ligands can be precisely and reversibly controlled. This enables the gentle, spatiotemporally precise capture and release of individual cells or delicate tissues without applying damaging mechanical or chemical forces, scaling down adhesion control to the molecular level for ultra-gentle biological manipulation.

ideas.title: Optothermally Responsive Nanointerface for Spatially Precise, Low-Force Cellular Manipulation
ideas.description: This system creates a biofunctionalized surface where cell adhesion is controlled by the reversible swelling/collapse of thermoresponsive polymer brushes, such as poly(N-isopropylacrylamide). Precise, low-power optical heating from a focused laser or patterned waveguide triggers localized adhesion changes without bulk heating. This allows for the selective release, patterning, or gentle peeling of individual cells or sub-cellular structures with forces below the threshold for membrane damage. The approach decouples the adhesion switch from harsh chemical or global thermal stimuli, enabling dynamic, non-destructive handling of sensitive biological specimens.

ideas.title: Temporally Programmable Hierarchical Assembly with Shape-Memory Stamps
ideas.description: This research develops a novel hierarchical assembly system using stamps composed of shape memory polymers (SMPs) with embedded dual-scale relief structures. The stamp's geometry is not static; its macro-scale curvature and micro/nano-scale surface features can be independently and sequentially activated via precise thermal control. This allows a single stamp to first pick up a large structure with a flat, adhesive conformation, then, upon heating, reconfigure to also pick up smaller components onto specific sites of the larger one, and finally release the entire multi-scale assembly upon cooling. This creates a dynamic, one-tool manufacturing process for complex 3D micro-systems that was previously impossible with passive stamps requiring multiple, aligned tools.

ideas.title: Field-Programmable Heterogeneous Integration via Stimuli-Responsive Transfer Printing
ideas.description: This research fuses reversible, stimuli-responsive adhesion with multi-material transfer printing to create a novel platform for field-programmable heterogeneous integration. The core innovation is the use of temperature- or electric-field-sensitive adhesives at the interfaces between printed components and a target substrate, allowing for the selective, non-destructive release and re-adhesion of specific device elements after initial assembly. This enables the dynamic reconfiguration of complex electronic systems—such as sensor arrays, RF circuits, or wearable devices—for functional upgrades, component replacement, or in-situ repair without damaging other integrated parts. The result is a transformative manufacturing paradigm that yields electronic devices which are not merely assembled, but are inherently adaptable and fault-tolerant throughout their operational lifetime.

ideas.title: Active Conformal Stamping for Macroscopic Assembly with Microscale Compliance
ideas.description: This redesigned approach uses a programmable stamping head featuring a dense array of independently controllable micro-actuators to assemble centimeter-scale components. Each micro-actuator adjusts in real-time based on surface topology feedback, applying locally optimal pressure to ensure complete, uniform contact across the bonding interface. This active compliance accommodates part misalignment, roughness, and differential thermal expansion that would cripple a rigid stamp. The result is a robust, scalable assembly technique capable of creating strong, defect-free macroscopic structures from pre-fabricated functional blocks.

ideas.title: Use shape memory polymers with photothermal dopants for optically controlled stamp geometry

In the analogous field, photothermal nanoparticles are irradiated to generate localized heat for precise therapeutic action. The adapted research idea employs this principle by incorporating photothermal dopants into a shape memory polymer stamp. When exposed to a patterned or scanned light source, these dopants generate heat, triggering a localized shape change in the polymer. This enables optically controlled, rapid, and spatially selective modulation of contact and adhesion, offering a non-contact method for dynamic stamp control in applications like transfer printing or adaptive gripping.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop Spatially Adaptive De-Adhesion for Precision Repair
ideas.description: This system enables reliable, residue-free material removal by directly confronting the challenge of interfacial heterogeneity. It uses embedded sensors to create a real-time map of adhesion strength across the target interface, identifying weak points and potential failure zones. An array of micro-actuators then applies a spatially and temporally tailored combination of debonding stimuli, propagating a clean separation front. This ensures complete, controlled removal without damage to the underlying substrate, making it robust for microelectronic repair, composite material rework, and biological tissue delamination.

ideas.title: Parallel Micro-Transfer Molding via Coordinated Reservoir Stamping
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the throughput limitation by enabling parallel, multi-material deposition. A structured elastomeric stamp, featuring an array of micro-molds, is aligned and brought into contact with a complementary array of pre-filled micro-reservoirs on a donor substrate, allowing all molds to fill simultaneously via capillary action. The stamp, now loaded with material in each mold, is then transferred and reversibly bonded to the target substrate for release. This decouples the slow, sequential filling step from the placement step, enabling rapid patterning of complex material combinations in a single transfer cycle.

ideas.title: A Hierarchical, Multi-Scale Assembly Protocol for Printed Electronics
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of integrating printed structures by introducing a hierarchical, multi-scale assembly protocol inspired by protein folding in structural biology. The analogous problem of building a complex, functional 3D biological machine from a 1D amino acid sequence is solved through hierarchical folding and assembly (primary, secondary, tertiary, quaternary structure). We adapt this solution by developing a transfer printing methodology where 2D printed elements are first formed into freestanding 3D functional modules (e.g., folded silicon membranes for logic, rolled micro-supercapacitors for power). These pre-assembled, heterogeneous modules are then integrated onto a final system substrate via a final transfer printing step, where designed interfacial materials enable both mechanical bonding and electrical interconnection. This approach decouples the fabrication of optimal sub-components from their system-level integration, enabling more complex and high-performance printed electronic systems than direct monolithic printing allows.

ideas.title: Closed-loop adaptive stamping for dynamic material compensation in microstructuring
ideas.description: This research develops a smart stamping system that dynamically adjusts its surface geometry during the imprinting process to compensate for real-time material behavior. It uses integrated sensors to monitor key process variables like local pressure and material flow, feeding this data to a control algorithm that actuates a reconfigurable stamp surface. This approach ensures the fidelity of the transferred microstructure across varying process conditions and within-material inhomogeneities, moving beyond static designs. The system will be validated across polymers, metals, and soft biological hydrogels to establish adaptive correction protocols for each material class.

ideas.title: Stochastic Non-Contact Assembly via Environmental Steering
ideas.description: This research direction develops a hybrid assembly platform that uses non-contact force fields (acoustic, magnetic, or optical) as the primary manipulation tool, but relinquishes fine-grained, real-time control of each field node. Instead, the system employs a population of simple, stochastic microrobotic units or dynamic field patterns whose collective behavior is shaped by a pre-programmed or adaptive environmental landscape. This landscape is engineered so that the inherent randomness and non-contact interactions of the units lead, with high probability, to the desired deterministic assembly outcome, such as the correct placement of micro-components. This merges the robustness of stochastic, environment-guided control with the precision of non-contact manipulation, enabling scalable parallel assembly without complex feedback for each individual element.

ideas.title: Optical Trapping and Sorting via Programmable Acoustic Holograms
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that deterministic assembly requires physical contact or grippers. By drawing an analogy from optical physics and biophysics, where optical tweezers use focused laser beams to trap and manipulate microscopic objects via gradient forces, we propose an adapted solution using acoustics. The analogous solution of creating a non-contact force field is adapted by developing a system of programmable ultrasonic transducer arrays capable of generating dynamic acoustic holograms—precise 3D pressure fields. These fields act as 'acoustic tweezers,' exerting controlled radiation forces to selectively pick, sort, and place micro-objects without any physical contact, enabling assembly of materials irrespective of optical properties.

ideas.title: Macro-assembly via Programmable Jig and Fixture Systems
ideas.description: This idea scales up assembly by adapting solutions from industrial manufacturing. The analogous problem of assembling complex, large-scale mechanical structures (like aircraft wings or car frames) with high precision is solved using modular jigs and fixtures—reconfigurable frameworks that physically constrain parts into the correct position for bonding. The adapted research idea applies this principle to build centimeter-scale or larger functional structures from smaller components. A programmable 'macro-jig' system is developed, featuring a scalable array of actuators that form a customizable 3D scaffold. Pre-fabricated functional blocks (sensors, actuators, compute units) are then precisely located within this scaffold via guided placement, enabling the rapid, accurate, and repeatable assembly of macroscopic, heterogeneous systems from libraries of micro- or mesoscale parts.

ideas.title: Material-adaptive hybrid electrostatic-vacuum chuck for universal substrate handling
ideas.description: This system overcomes the material limitation of pure electrostatic chucks by integrating a dual clamping technology head. It first characterizes the substrate's electrical properties to determine if it is conductive or insulating. For conductive materials, a bipolar ESC provides precise, particle-free pre-alignment and holding. For insulating materials, a vacuum-based Bernoulli chuck gently levitates and secures the substrate without physical contact. This adaptive approach guarantees reliable pick-up, alignment, and release across the full spectrum of materials used in advanced packaging and semiconductor manufacturing.

ideas.title: Electrostatic Pre-Alignment via Programmable Fringe-Field Patterns
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of using electrostatic chucks for pre-alignment and holding by drawing an analogy from optical tweezers in biophysics. In that field, shaped laser beams create complex intensity patterns to trap and rotate particles for alignment without contact. The adapted solution is to design electrostatic chucks with segmented or patterned electrodes capable of generating programmable, non-uniform fringe-field patterns. These tailored electrostatic fields produce controlled lateral dielectrophoretic forces, which can gently rotate and precisely pre-align a wafer or substrate as it approaches the chuck, correcting minor angular misalignment before the full electrostatic clamping force is applied for secure holding during the transfer process.

ideas.title: Adaptive Micro-Gripper Array for Deterministic Transfer Printing
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from macro-scale automated warehousing to the challenge of wafer-level integration. In logistics, Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems (AS/RS) use grids of independent, computer-controlled shuttles to rapidly and precisely manage inventory. The adapted research idea proposes implementing transfer printing not with a single large-area stamp, but with a scalable, programmable array of independent micro-gripper transfer units. Each micro-gripper would be individually addressable to pick and place specific micro-components from a source wafer to a target substrate. Coordinated by a central control system that manages placement sequences and avoids collisions, this adaptive array would enable high-throughput, parallel, and heterogeneous deterministic assembly at the wafer scale, overcoming the limitations of rigid, pattern-specific stamps.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop Transfer Printing with In-Situ Interconnect Formation for Reliable Heterogeneous Systems
ideas.description: This research demonstrates the assembly of complete, multi-material electronic systems via a transfer printing process that directly addresses the critical interconnect challenge. A closed-loop vision system provides real-time feedback for micron-scale alignment of each transferred component, while an integrated localized energy source (e.g., laser, micro-heater) simultaneously forms a permanent, low-resistance electrical and mechanical bond at the contact points. This in-situ bonding ensures immediate functional connectivity and structural stability after each transfer step, preventing later failure. The result is a scalable fabrication method for complex devices where system yield and performance are guaranteed by the sequential, verified integration of each heterogeneous component.

ideas.title: Perceive and adapt to dynamic tissue heterogeneity with embedded real-time sensing
ideas.description: This approach designs control strategies that actively perceive, rather than assume, the complex and varying properties of tissues and fluids. It embeds miniaturized, multi-modal sensor arrays onto the navigating device to create a real-time spatiotemporal map of mechanical stiffness, fluid viscosity, and chemical gradients. The control algorithm uses this live sensory stream to dynamically adjust navigation parameters like propulsion force, steering angle, and gait, enabling adaptation to unforeseen local conditions. This transforms the system from one that is robust to expected variations into one that is adaptive to measured, real-world heterogeneity.

ideas.title: Photocleavable Adhesive Stamp for Spatially Programmed Debonding
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from synthetic chemistry, where photolabile protecting groups enable the precise, light-triggered removal of blocking agents, to the original inverse stamping problem. The analogous solution uses light of a specific wavelength to trigger a clean, irreversible chemical cleavage. Adapted to the materials debonding context, a stamp is coated with an adhesive layer containing photolabile moieties within its polymer network or at the adhesive interface. Applying the stamp bonds it to a material or component. Subsequent patterned irradiation (e.g., through a mask, via laser scanning) then severs the photolabile bonds in designated areas, drastically reducing adhesion strength only in those zones. This allows for the selective, precise, and programmable removal of material—like a micro-component for repair or a defective layer—mimicking the function of a stamp that removes rather than deposits, scaled for applications from microelectronics disassembly to structured material editing.

ideas.title: Adaptive Optics Feedback for Microrobot Navigation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the closed-loop correction framework from astronomical adaptive optics to the challenge of real-time microrobot control within the body. In astronomy, adaptive optics systems continuously sense atmospheric distortion with a wavefront sensor and use a deformable mirror to correct it, enabling clear telescopic imaging. The analogous solution for microrobotics is to treat the dynamic in vivo environment (like blood flow or tissue movement) as the 'distortion' to be corrected. By integrating advanced medical imaging (MRI, ultrasound) as the real-time 'sensor' of the microrobot's state and its surroundings, a high-bandwidth control system can be designed. This system would calculate the discrepancy between the intended and actual microrobot trajectory and instantly apply corrective steering forces (e.g., via magnetic fields or focused ultrasound), creating a stable, closed-loop navigation platform that actively compensates for physiological noise and disturbances.

ideas.title: Ambient-Energy-Harvesting Microrobots with Event-Driven Autonomy
ideas.description: This research develops fully autonomous microrobots that overcome the critical energy bottleneck by eliminating reliance on finite internal power stores. Instead, the robots are equipped with integrated microscale harvesters that convert ambient energy—from vibrations, light, thermal differentials, or radio waves—into usable power. An onboard, event-driven intelligence module remains in an ultra-low-power state, activating only to process sensor input or execute maneuvers when sufficient harvested energy is available. This design enables potentially indefinite operation in complex, unstructured environments, making true long-term microrobotic autonomy feasible.
